entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Group Reporting: Handling Standard and Temporal Currency Translation for Same FS Item
entry.content:
Hello SAP Guru's

For the current SAP S/4HANA Group Reporting on-premise 2023 requirement, we need to support two currency translation methods:

  1. Standard Translation Method
  2. Temporal Translation Method

From the SAP GUI configuration side, we understand that different translation methods and selections can be created based on the business requirement and assigned accordingly.

For example, under the Temporal Translation Method, certain non-monetary FS items may need a different translation treatment. The periodic movement may need to be translated using the applicable month-end rate and then combined with the opening balance to arrive at the closing balance.

Our understanding is that the translation method assigned to the Consolidation Unit will drive how the currency translation is calculated in Group Reporting.

However, we would like to understand how this should be handled from the Fiori app “Define FS Items” perspective.

Since the FS item master allows only one translation attribute or translation-related setting, how does the system determine which treatment to apply when the same FS item is used under both the Standard Translation Method and the Temporal Translation Method?

We also want to avoid creating separate FS items only to support different translation methods, as that may not be the right approach from an IFRS reporting perspective.

Could you please advise the recommended standard SAP approach in SAP S/4HANA Group Reporting on-premise 2023 to handle this requirement in a clean, controlled, and IFRS-aligned way?

Thank you in advance.

NavT

entry.title: Cannot create Party Entitled to Dispose
entry.content:
I am creating a new company code and have gotten most of the steps complete. I am stuck on creating the Party Entitled to Dispose. I have created the Business Partner exactly the same as the business partner for the original company code. When I go to Set Warehouse Address and add the new warehouse in, I get the error message "The system can't determine the party entitled to dispose." 

Old Company code = 1010. New Company Code = 2010

Things I have checked: 

  • Warehouse is EWM Managed
  • Storage Locations are attached to warehouse
  • Company code 2010 exists and is active
  • Country, currency, and address are maintained
  • Fiscal year variant assigned
  • Posting period variant assigned
  • 2010 → same controlling area as 1010
  • Every plant used in logistics is assigned to 2010
  • No plant is left assigned to 1010 accidentally
  • No duplicate plant assignment across incompatible company codes
  • Storage locations exist under correct plant
  • PED exists for warehouse
  • PED is assigned to:
    • Warehouse number
    • Plant
    • Storage locations (if required)
  • PED is ACTIVE (not draft / inactive)
  • PED is linked to correct organizational unit (often shipping point or warehouse org unit)
  • BP has both Supplier and Customer role
  • Company code segment exists for 2010
  • Purchasing / shipping data is maintained
  • BP is not missing “plant-level” logistics roles

I know I am missing something obvious/simple but I just can't figure it out. Any help is greatly appreciated. 

Vicki

entry.title: SAP Integration Suite Runtime APIs
entry.content:
Hello Team,

I am currently working on leveraging SAP Integration Suite Runtime APIs and have been exploring available documentation and resources.

To ensure alignment with SAP-recommended approaches and avoid any deviations from best practices, I would like to request your guidance on the following areas:

  1. Official References
    • Links to authentic and up-to-date SAP documentation for Runtime APIs.
    • Any relevant API specifications or SAP Notes that cover this topic.
  2. Recommended Approach
    • Best practices for consuming Runtime APIs in enterprise scenarios.
    • Guidance on authentication strategies, error handling, and scalability considerations.
  3. Implementation Insights
    • Any sample use cases, integration patterns, or reference implementations.
    • Recommendations based on typical customer usage.
  4. Configuration Considerations
    • Key prerequisites, roles, and configuration steps to ensure correct setup.
    • Any known limitations or important considerations.
  5. Troubleshooting & Monitoring
    • Common challenges observed and suggested resolutions.
    • Recommended approach for logging, monitoring, and diagnostics.

I have already started reviewing the available materials but wanted to validate my approach against SAP's recommended standards and see if there are any additional resources I should refer to.

entry.title: How to know memory used by DP Agent in queries from SAP Datasphere (Remote Tables)
entry.content:
Dear SAP Support,

I’d like to know how to determine the amount of memory used by different remote table queries using DP Agent. I’ve checked the logs, but this information isn’t there. It’s also not in the HANA Cloud database underlying Datasphere—or perhaps I haven’t looked at the right view or table.

We’ve seen this link for making the calculation, but I’d like to know if increasing the DP Agent log level would allow us to determine this information precisely, as well as the peaks it reaches.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: How to know memory used by DP Agent in queries from S...
entry.content:
Hello,

We have discussed this scenario with the Product Area Lead for SAP HANA Smart Data Integration (SDI).

The information on memory utilization for a specific table query is not available.

Instead, SDI provides the following sizing guides to help you decide the most suitable agent size. Your DBA for the source system should be familiar with your data and help make this decision based on the information outlined in the documents (number of tables, tables size, modification rate, etc.).

Guide 1: Replication Table Categorization https://help.sap.com/docs/HANA_SMART_DATA_INTEGRATION/bf2f0282053648f8a1ef873e65ded81a/772c00dcd2674c60877b2f6e24708254.html?locale=en-US&version=LATEST

Guide 2: Template-Based Sizing Approach https://help.sap.com/docs/HANA_SMART_DATA_INTEGRATION/bf2f0282053648f8a1ef873e65ded81a/b9f44b8a45c4421eb554a8345d8debe0.html?locale=en-US&version=LATEST

Best regards,

SAP Support

entry.title: Re: Batch traceability for Finished and Semifinished Mate...
entry.content:
Hi,

This is a common pharma formulation challenge — you need batch-level traceability on finished goods, but your material types are set up differently. Here's how to approach it in SAP Business One:

The core issue: Finished goods (Fert) and semi-finished (Halb) materials are not batch-managed by default in SAP B1. Only raw materials carry batch numbers, which breaks your genealogy chain from ingredients → formulation → FG.

Two paths to solve it:

Option A — Enable Batch Management on Item Master
Go to Item Master Data → Logistics tab → check "Batch Management" and "Manage Item By" (batch numbers). This works if your production process genuinely creates distinct, traceable batches at the FG level. Your goods issue against production orders will then consume specific raw material batches and generate corresponding FG batch numbers.

Option B — Documentary Batches
If you don't have physical batch separation but need traceability, use the Documentary Batch feature (user-defined). This lets you assign logical batch IDs to finished goods without physical separation. Be aware: availability checks and QM inspections are not fully supported for documentary batches in SAP B1 — this matters for pharma shops under Schedule M compliance.

Pro tip for Hyderabad pharma shops: If you're operating under USFDA or WHO-GMP environments (common in Genome Valley and Patancheru clusters), Option A is almost always the right call — documentary batches won't survive a regulatory audit. Budget the extra 2–3 days of go-live preparation to get batch management right from Day 1.

For the BMBC report (Batch Information Cockpit), the genealogy tree will show you component batches → production order → FG batch — but only if all materials in the chain are batch-managed.

Happy to clarify any step.

— Venkata Siva Reddy Polu, TEKROI
SAP B1 implementation for Hyderabad pharma and manufacturing SMBs

entry.title: Re: SAP Sapphire 2026: What the announcements really mean for ECC and S/4HANA customers
entry.content: Good summary, Vitaly. As one point of clarification, when Vitaly or SAP refers to "S/4HANA On-Premise," they are referring to an SAP non-subscription license, which has nothing to do with the hosting location being on-premises. SAP Cloud ERP Private Edition/RISE, Customer Data Center is available for hosting at a customer's premises, and there is no difference in access to new AI features between the hosting options. Many customers prefer using the term, "S/4HANA Perpetual License" as it avoids confusion with hosting location yet clearly indicates non-cloud subscription. Please don't let this trivial clarification distract from this excellent summary!

entry.title: Re: PLM solutions from SAP Events 2026
entry.content: Thank you for putting this together — incredibly useful to have all SAP events in one place! Really looking forward to seeing what Q3 and Q4 bring as the details fill in. For those of us in the extended SAP ecosystem (I'm currently focused on EWM/Supply Chain), events like SAP Supply Chain Connect in Las Vegas and the SAP NOW AI Tour stops are particularly exciting to watch. Will definitely be bookmarking this and checking back. Great work keeping this updated regularly!

igor 6176 67.3 2.3 222784 187948 pts/0 R+ 13:42 0:07 /usr/libexec/gcc/x86_64-pc-linux-gnu/11.3.0/cc1plus -quiet -I . -I ../../libpropertypages -I .. -I ../../dbinterface -I ../../libshapeframework -I ../../libfieldswindow -I ../../libhelpers -I /usr/local/lib/wx/include/gtk3-unicode-3.3 -I /usr/local/include/wx-3.3 -I /usr/include/gtk-3.0 -I /usr/include/pango-1.0 -I /usr/include/glib-2.0 -I /usr/lib64/glib-2.0/include -I /usr/include/sysprof-4 -I /usr/lib64/libffi/include -I /usr/include/harfbuzz -I /usr/include/freetype2 -I /usr/include/libmount -I /usr/include/blkid -I /usr/include/fribidi -I /usr/include/uuid -I /usr/include/cairo -I /usr/include/pixman-1 -I /usr/include/libpng16 -I /usr/include/gdk-pixbuf-2.0 -I /usr/include/gio-unix-2.0 -I /usr/include/sysprof-3 -I /usr/include/atk-1.0 -I /usr/include/at-spi2-atk/2.0 -I /usr/include/dbus-1.0 -I /usr/lib64/dbus-1.0/include -I /usr/include/at-spi-2.0 -MD .libs/libpropertypages_la-tableprimarykey.d -MF .deps/libpropertypages_la-tableprimarykey.Tpo -MP -MT libpropertypages_la-tableprimarykey.lo -D_GNU_SOURCE -D_REENTRANT -D HAVE_CONFIG_H -D _FILE_OFFSET_BITS=64 -D WXUSINGDLL -D __WXGTK__ -D PIC ../../libpropertypages/tableprimarykey.cpp -quiet -dumpdir .libs/ -dumpbase libpropertypages_la-tableprimarykey.cpp -dumpbase-ext .cpp -mtune=generic -march=x86-64 -g -O0 -fPIC -o /tmp/ccEzVYDP.s
igor 6249 70.7 1.3 145316 104576 pts/0 R+ 13:42 0:02 /usr/libexec/gcc/x86_64-pc-linux-gnu/11.3.0/cc1plus -quiet -I . -I ../../libpropertypages -I .. -I ../../dbinterface -I ../../libshapeframework -I ../../libfieldswindow -I ../../libhelpers -I /usr/local/lib/wx/include/gtk3-unicode-3.3 -I /usr/local/include/wx-3.3 -I /usr/include/gtk-3.0 -I /usr/include/pango-1.0 -I /usr/include/glib-2.0 -I /usr/lib64/glib-2.0/include -I /usr/include/sysprof-4 -I /usr/lib64/libffi/include -I /usr/include/harfbuzz -I /usr/include/freetype2 -I /usr/include/libmount -I /usr/include/blkid -I /usr/include/fribidi -I /usr/include/uuid -I /usr/include/cairo -I /usr/include/pixman-1 -I /usr/include/libpng16 -I /usr/include/gdk-pixbuf-2.0 -I /usr/include/gio-unix-2.0 -I /usr/include/sysprof-3 -I /usr/include/atk-1.0 -I /usr/include/at-spi2-atk/2.0 -I /usr/include/dbus-1.0 -I /usr/lib64/dbus-1.0/include -I /usr/include/at-spi-2.0 -MD .libs/libpropertypages_la-designgeneral.d -MF .deps/libpropertypages_la-designgeneral.Tpo -MP -MT libpropertypages_la-designgeneral.lo -D_GNU_SOURCE -D_REENTRANT -D HAVE_CONFIG_H -D _FILE_OFFSET_BITS=64 -D WXUSINGDLL -D __WXGTK__ -D PIC ../../libpropertypages/designgeneral.cpp -quiet -dumpdir .libs/ -dumpbase libpropertypages_la-designgeneral.cpp -dumpbase-ext .cpp -mtune=generic -march=x86-64 -g -O0 -fPIC -o /tmp/cclE9n7e.s
root 6267 0.0 0.0 8912 2504 pts/1 S+ 13:42 0:00 grep --colour=auto ase
[1]+ Done startserver -f RUN_REINCLOUD

[/code]

 

I though the server started successfully, but when trying to connect with dbisql, I;m getting:

 

[code]

Could not connect to the server.

Connection refused.

Ensure that the name in the "Server name" field is correct and that the server has been started.
JZ006: Caught IOException: java.net.ConnectException: error 111 - Connection refused (Connection refused) (local port 33652 to address 127.0.0.1 (localhost), remote port 5000 to address 127.0.0.1 (localhost))
SQLCODE=0
SQLSTATE=JZ006

Connection parameters:
User=sa
Password=*********
ENG=REINCLOUD

[/code]

 

What is wrong? How do I start the server and make sure its running...

 

Thank you.

P.S.: I'm also interested in fixing the locale warning I saw on Terminal...

P.P.S.:

[code]

IgorReinCloud /opt/sap # ls -la SYSAM-2_0/licenses/
total 12
drwxrwxr-x 2 root root 4096 May 8 13:43 .
drwxrwxr-x 6 root root 4096 May 8 13:33 ..
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root 0 Jun 16 2025 empty
-rw-rw-r-- 1 root root 88 May 8 13:43 SYBASE.opt
IgorReinCloud /opt/sap #

[/code]

 

I don't understand this at all. It ran after install but not after I reboot?

 

Could someone please explain?

 

Thank you.

entry.title: Integrating SAP BTP DMS with SAP Cloud ALM to upload external files in SAP Cloud ALM, using Boosters
entry.content:
During a migration from SAP Solution Manager to SAP Cloud ALM, we needed an efficient way to manage and access project documents in the new environment. Integrating SAP BTP Document Management Service (DMS) provided a scalable solution for centralized document handling and seamless file uploads within SAP Cloud ALM. This approach not only streamlined document management but also improved accessibility for project teams. As there are limited resources explaining how to configure DMS using boosters, this blog outlines the steps we followed to simplify the setup and achieve a smooth integration.

     Prerequisites:

  1. Create an SAP Cloud ALM subaccount in SAP BTP.
  2. Configure the subaccount for SAP Cloud ALM implementation.
  3. Verify that the system is ready for use in SAP Cloud ALM implementation scenarios.
    You can follow my blog to configure SAP Cloud ALM for Implementation of your SAP S/4HANA private cloud landscape:
    How to Configure SAP Cloud ALM for Implementation ... - SAP Community

    Overview of the setup:

    Before we begin, here’s a quick overview of what we’ll be doing in this setup. These steps will help you prepare the SAP BTP environment, configure the required services, and connect it with SAP Cloud ALM for document management.

    Step 1: Create BTP Subaccount

    We first create a separate space in SAP BTP to host and manage the Document Management Service.

    Step 2: Configure Entitlements and Services

    Next, we enable the required services so that the Document Management functionality can be used.

    Step 3: Set Up DMS Using Boosters

    We then use boosters to quickly set up the required components without manual configuration.

    Step 4: Create Destinations in SAP Cloud ALM

    Finally, we connect SAP Cloud ALM with DMS so documents can be uploaded and accessed seamlessly.

  1. Create BTP subaccount for SAP BTP-DMS:

We need to perform this step, to create a dedicated environment where all DMS-related configurations and services can be managed separately and securely.
To perform this step, log in to the SAP BTP Global Account with Global Administrator authorizations, and click on the “Create” button to set up a new subaccount for SAP BTP Document Management Service (DMS):

 

A popup as below will appear, enter name, Region, Subdomain name & description and click on create.
A subaccount for SAP BTP DMS is created.



Note: The subdomain name uniquely identifies your SAP BTP subaccount and is used to generate service URLs and authentication endpoints. It should be chosen carefully, as it cannot be changed later.

2. Create the Entitlements in the subaccount and subscribe to the services for SAP DMS:

We need to perform this step to enable and activate the required SAP services, so the Document Management functionality becomes available for use.

To perform this step, Goto the above created subaccount for DMS and click on Entitlements->Edit->Add service plan:

Search for:
  • Document Management Service, Integration Option
  • Document Management Service, Repository
    Select the required plan (e.g., standard)
    Click Add Service Plans → Save

Note: You do not need to subscribe to DMS Application in this setup, as the integration is specifically between SAP Cloud ALM and SAP BTP Document Management Service for document management purposes.

3. Using boosters to create the instance, service key, and environment for the SAP DMS subaccount:

SAP provides an easy option to quickly set up all necessary components (like instances and keys) without doing each configuration manually, saving time and effort by using boosters.
In this blog, we will demonstrate how to use boosters to set up the SAP BTP DMS subaccount.
Goto the SAP BTP Global account and click on Boosters, search for DMS boosters, you will get the following option:
Click on the start button:



The following popup will open and will check prerequisites as a first step:

2nd step is to select repository, choose the Internal Repository if you want SAP BTP DMS to store and manage documents within SAP itself (recommended for most SAP Cloud ALM scenarios). Select File Share Repository only if you need to connect to an external storage system (e.g., Google Drive or other third-party repositories) for document storage.

Next step is to select the subaccount created for SAP DMS:

Final step is to add users, the person who is running these steps is an administrator, apart from that, if you want to add any other person, you can give the id under administrators and select Next step and Finish:

Wait for all the steps to be completed and then Navigate to the subaccount, you will be able to see, Instance created with service key and environment created too in the subaccount for DMS: Instances and Subscriptions:

 


Download the service key to create Destination in the next step.

4. In the SAP Cloud ALM subaccount, create destinations for the SAP BTP DMS subaccount using the service key credentials to integrate SAP CALM and SAP DMS.

We perform this step, to connect SAP Cloud ALM with DMS so they can communicate and allow documents to be uploaded and accessed seamlessly.

To perform this step, switch account to go to the SAP CALM subaccount and click on Connectivity->Destinations:

Click on create and select "From scratch":

Enter the following details in the fields to create Destination for SAP BTP DMS:

Field Value
NameCALM_DMS_Integration
TypeHTTP
DescriptionDescription for Destination
Proxy TypeInternet
AuthenticationOAuth2ClientCredentials
URLREPLACE_WITH_SERVICE_KEY_URI
Token Service URLREPLACE_WITH_UAA_TOKEN_URL/oauth/token
Client IDFrom the service key created for SAP BTP DMS
Client SecretFrom the service key created for SAP BTP DMS

Click on save and test the connection. Check after 5-10 minutes the external Files field in Document services in SAP CALM should have upload option:

You can now start uploading the Documents and assign it to your Requirements & User stories in SAP CALM.

In this blog, we explored how to integrate SAP BTP Document Management Service (DMS) with SAP Cloud ALM using boosters, enabling seamless upload and management of external files in SAP Cloud ALM. We covered the key prerequisites, subaccount setup, service enablement, and the creation of destinations to establish a secure connection between SAP BTP DMS and SAP Cloud ALM.

With this setup, you can enhance document handling capabilities within SAP Cloud ALM and streamline your implementation processes. I hope this guide helps you implement the integration smoothly. Feel free to share your feedback or ask any questions in the comments section—I’d be happy to help.

entry.title: Re: Building Custom Joule Capabilities: Getting Started
entry.content:
Hi @david_bizer - Thank you so much for this post.

It's been so hard to find simple and well structured information about Joule usually and yours achieved that with such clarity.

It would be awesome and very helpful if you could post any tutorial using the same approach, but related to how to test Joule as a complement to this series.

The SAP wiki (https://help.sap.com/docs/joule/joule-development-guide-4b327297dce247fcb88a5f5bfeea97a1/test-capability) offers some knowledge related to this topic, but I find it so useless and vague that I believe no one can really take advantage of it.

entry.title: CPI Process Colorizer and Where used global search - Chrome extensions with the help of Vibe coding
entry.content:
When I started working on all of this, I was after two things:

  1. Make my life easier when refactoring complex flows with no proper documentation (Yes, that's happens).
  2. To see how far the unhinged genius of vibe coding can take me, considering how deeply AI started to integrate with daily routine. (Spoiler alert: pretty far. "Away" or not - it's discussible 😁)

Because once you see flow with 30+ subprocesses with names "Local integration Process 1 .. N", you inevitably start asking the Universe some questions. For example about:

  and  

 

 

Let's start with "Where used" global search as appetizer.

 

CPI Where Used rmbc

Link to Chrome Web Storage. Link to source code at Codeberg.

The idea is to find all mentions of selected text within all flows. It might greatly help, for example: when you are shifting from one Cloud connector to another and you need to change all Location Ids, urls, credentials and etc. and you have no idea which flow has it.

Select text on a page at your CPI tenant (both CF and Neo), right-click, choose "CPI Where Used rmbc" (rmbc = right mouse button click) and the extension opens a new tab with all iFlows where that exact text appears (checking credentials, pgp keys, urls, jms queus, script names and etc. including search in default values and externalized configured params.).

Why contextMenus ? Simply because I have enough buttons from different extensions.

Spoiler


How it works:
The extension:
1. Uses the current CPI tenant URL and existing browser session cookies.
2. Fetches a CSRF token.
3. Loads all CPI packages.
4. Loads artifacts for each package.
5. Filters artifacts to "IFlow".
6. Downloads each "iFlow detail JSON".
7. Recursively searches all string, number and boolean values for the selected text.
8. Opens a result tab showing package, iFlow, JSON path and snippet.

Around 200 flows took a little more than a minute to retrieve and parse.

Since I wanted to avoid redownload everything in case of need of new search within small period of time - caching is introduced.

Caching:
Package/artifact metadata and iFlow detail JSON are cached per tenant for 15 minutes inside the extension service worker. It is not persisted in chrome.storage. So repeating a search shortly after the previous one should avoid much of the repeated downloading and waiting, unless Chrome stops the service worker or the cache expires.

Limitations:
It's obviously can't find dynamically built queue names, since those generated names do not exist within the flow metadata.

It does not unzip full exported packages. As a result it's not parsing inside groovy scripts and etc., so Extension will not find function "ABC" if it's only not mentioned in attributes.
Hm... Idea...

 

And now the "main dish":

 

CPI Colorizer and Process Panel

Link to Chrome Web Storage. Link to source code at Codeberg.

This one visually colorizes SAP CPI processes and subprocess call boxes and shows a searchable process panel from the BPMN model. And most important thing: it navigate you in the model via pan to selected process or its' step. You don't need frantically search in which subprocess, under which label desired script exist.

How it works:
Two editor-only enhancements:

1. Process colorization
The extension reads the BPMN model of the currently opened iFlow and builds a process color matrix. Main Integration Processes, Local Integration Processes, and local process-call boxes are colored consistently. A call box receives the same color as the subprocess it calls, making process relationships easier to understand visually.

Spoiler

2. Searchable process panel
The extension injects an “iFlow Processes” panel into the CPI left navigation area. The panel lists processes, subprocesses, exception subprocesses, and steps in flow order. It includes search, expand all, collapse all and BPMN reload controls. Searching can find step names, process names, scripts, mapping names, tooltips, and configured step attributes.

Spoiler
   

Panel rows are clickable. Clicking a process or step pans/selects the corresponding object in the CPI model and highlights it with a temporary pulse outline.
8Mb gif under the spoiler.

Spoiler

 Honorable mention: gif was created by "Capture to a Gif" extension. Link to Chrome Web Storage

The extension includes a browser popup where users can configure:
- whether Color/Panel is enabled by default
- process/subprocess positioning
- step positioning

Spoiler

It's needed cause everyone is working under different screen resolution and has their own preferences. You can always toogle colorization and panel injection by floating button.

 

Now I will go full nerd mode with the code comments (functions with more than 10 lines would be under the spoiler)... You can skip it to PS: if you want.

What's going on at the editor page:

Side panel:
Custom Panel is injected as the last <li> item in a list of <ul id="shell--navigationList" ...>:

<ul id="shell--navigationList" ...>
  ...
  <li id="cpi-colorizer-side-panel" data-cpi-colorizer="process-panel">
    <!-- extension panel content -->
  </li>
</ul>

It was done intentionally, so it participates in the same navigation scrolling area as the native menu entries. The panel content itself does not use its own max-height or internal scrollbar.

Tried to inject it as separate block with fixed height and separate scroll bar, but with small screen resolution it would be very hard to use and scroll bars would be everywhere, since we would "eat" the space of standard Menu. So moved to "<ul id="shell--navigationList" ...>".

But in this case a bug was encountered: if expand/collapse standard menu injection is flickering. Reason is that SAPUI5 rerenders the list and temporarily removes injected <li>, later the watcher inserts it again.

setInterval(() => {
  ensureSidePanelInjected();
}, 1500);

To make it smooth, it was done via MutationObserver (provides the ability to watch for changes being made to the DOM tree) .

const host =
  document.getElementById("shell--sideNavigationMenu-Flexible-Content")
  || document.getElementById("shell--navigationList")?.parentElement;
// then
sidePanelObserver.observe(host, {
  childList: true,
  subtree: true
});

Checking if <div id="shell--sideNavigationMenu-Flexible-Content"> is available. If not - fallback to <ul id="shell--navigationList">.
When SAPUI5 expands/collapses menu items or rerenders the left navigation, the observer calls

function scheduleSidePanelEnsure() {
  if (sidePanelEnsureRaf) return;

  sidePanelEnsureRaf = requestAnimationFrame(() => {
    sidePanelEnsureRaf = null;
    ensureSidePanel();
  });
}

Executing function ensureSidePanel()

Spoiler
function ensureSidePanel() {
  const navigationList = document.getElementById("shell--navigationList");
  if (!navigationList) return false;

  let panel = document.getElementById(PANEL_ID);
  const wasCreated = !panel;
  if (!panel) {
    panel = createSidePanel();
    navigationList.appendChild(panel);
  } else if (panel.parentElement !== navigationList) {
    // SAPUI5 can rerender the left navigation. If preserved panel is still
    // present but no longer inside the current navigation <ul>, move it back.
    navigationList.appendChild(panel);
  }

  if (currentModel) {
    // Do not repaint the whole panel on every observer check.
    // Only ensure the panel exists after SAPUI5 rerenders the left navigation.
    const body = document.getElementById(PANEL_BODY_ID);
    if (wasCreated || !body || body.childElementCount === 0) {
      renderSidePanelTree(getSidePanelSearchValue());
    }
  } else {
    loadSidePanelModel();
  }

  return true;
}

But the biggest time eater was navigation and positioning. 


Navigation and positioning:
First idea was to navigate SVG nodes

[id*="CallActivity_63"]
[data-sap-ui*="CallActivity_63"]
[aria-label*="CallActivity_63"]

Then just scroll into view

element.scrollIntoView({
  block: "center",
  inline: "center"
});

And emulate mouse events

element.dispatchEvent(new MouseEvent("mousedown", { bubbles: true }));
element.dispatchEvent(new MouseEvent("mouseup", { bubbles: true }));
element.dispatchEvent(new MouseEvent("click", { bubbles: true }));

But that fails. Scrolling was not working at all and diagram was hysterically moved by emulating clicks.

The cause is that CPI has its own pan control with buttons

To find them we need in Chrome Dev Tools (F12) in console tab

[...document.querySelectorAll("*")]
  .filter(el => {
    const text = [
      el.getAttribute("title"),
      el.getAttribute("aria-label"),
      el.textContent
    ]
      .filter(Boolean)
      .join(" ")
      .toLowerCase();

    return text.includes("pan view");
  });

Dumping results to the AI helps a lot to solve the main problem: that clicking emulation not only trigger standard movement behavior but also triggering positioning of the bottom property panel, that changing the size of the box where diagram is.

So solution is "pan" first to the specific anchoring values and only after that "select". Starting this flow by

Spoiler
/**  Entry point for panel click navigation to a Process_* or SubProcess_* or step BPMN id. */
function focusRenderedModelElement(bpmnId) {
  const target = findRenderedModelElement(bpmnId);
  if (!target) {
    setSidePanelStatus(`Could not find rendered element ${bpmnId}. Try Reload BPMN model.`);
    return;
  }

  // Selection itself makes CPI/Galilei auto-pan the model. That auto-pan fights
  // with our own pan calculation, so navigation now does the opposite order:
  // first use Galilei's pan buttons to move the target close to our desired
  // viewport anchor, then select it. When the element is already visible in a
  // good position, CPI's selection has little or no reason to move the view.
  const runId = ++panelNavigationRunId;
  lastPanelNavigation = { bpmnId, timestamp: Date.now() };
  preAnchorThenSelectModelElement(bpmnId, runId);
}

 Then find the real SVG object like:

Spoiler
CallActivity_63  → BPMNShape_CallActivity_63
SubProcess_16    → BPMNShape_SubProcess_16
Process_1        → BPMNShape_Participant_Process_1
/**
 * Finds the exact rendered BPMNShape_* SVG group for a BPMN model id.
 */
function findRenderedModelElement(bpmnId) {
  const id = String(bpmnId || "");
  if (!id) return null;

  const diagramId = currentModel?.shapeDiagramIdById?.get(id);
  const candidateIds = uniqueStrings([
    diagramId,
    `BPMNShape_${id}`,
    normalizeProcessId(id) ? `BPMNShape_Participant_${id}` : ""
  ]);

  for (const candidateId of candidateIds) {
    const element = document.getElementById(candidateId);
    if (element && !isExtensionElement(element)) return element;
  }

  // Fallback only. Some CPI builds expose the id in sap-automation or other attributes.
  // Restrict to Galilei/SVG nodes so our own side-panel rows are never returned.
  const escapedId = cssEscape(id);
  return document.querySelector(
    `svg .sapGalileiSymbolNode[id$="${escapedId}"], ` +
    `svg g[sap-automation*="${escapedId}"], ` +
    `svg g[id*="${escapedId}"]`
  );
}

Key function is preAnchorThenSelectModelElement() that is moving view in steps with tick of 22 ms

Spoiler
/**
 * Pans the target into place first, then pulses and selects it in CPI.
 */
function preAnchorThenSelectModelElement(bpmnId, runId) {
  // Run enough pan passes before selecting so a single panel click can cover
  // far-away elements. Earlier versions only did a small number of pan steps,
  // so users had to click the same panel row repeatedly. Galilei pan buttons
  // update the SVG asynchronously, therefore each pass does one small pan and
  // waits briefly before measuring again. The short delay keeps long-distance
  // jumps responsive while still letting Galilei apply each pan step.
  let pass = 0;
  let stableNearAnchorTicks = 0;
  let lastPositionSignature = "";
  const maxPasses = 120;

  const tick = () => {
    if (runId !== panelNavigationRunId || lastPanelNavigation.bpmnId !== bpmnId) return;
    const target = findRenderedModelElement(bpmnId);
    if (!target) return;

    const signature = getRenderedElementAnchorSignature(bpmnId);
    if (signature && signature === lastPositionSignature) {
      stableNearAnchorTicks += isRenderedModelElementNearAnchor(bpmnId) ? 1 : 0;
    } else {
      stableNearAnchorTicks = isRenderedModelElementNearAnchor(bpmnId) ? 1 : 0;
      lastPositionSignature = signature;
    }

    if (stableNearAnchorTicks < 2 && pass < maxPasses) {
      anchorRenderedModelElementWithGalileiPan(bpmnId, { pulse: false, maxIterations: 1 });
      pass += 1;
      setTimeout(tick, 22);
      return;
    }

    const finalTarget = findRenderedModelElement(bpmnId) || target;
    pulseRenderedModelElement(finalTarget);
    dispatchSyntheticEditorClick(finalTarget);

    // Re-pulse after CPI opens the standard properties area. Do not pan after
    // selection: CPI may auto-adjust the viewport while selecting the object,
    // and a second extension pan would fight that internal movement.
    setTimeout(() => {
      if (runId !== panelNavigationRunId || lastPanelNavigation.bpmnId !== bpmnId) return;
      const selectedTarget = findRenderedModelElement(bpmnId);
      if (selectedTarget) pulseRenderedModelElement(selectedTarget);
    }, 450);
  };

  tick();
}

Then just check if we are close enough to selection event.

Spoiler
function anchorRenderedModelElementWithGalileiPan(bpmnId, options = {}) {
  const target = findRenderedModelElement(bpmnId);
  if (!(target instanceof Element)) return;

  const viewport = findGalileiViewport(target);
  const controls = findGalileiPanControls();
  if (!viewport || !controls.left || !controls.right || !controls.up || !controls.down) return;

  const maxIterations = Number.isFinite(options.maxIterations) ? options.maxIterations : 12;

  for (let i = 0; i < maxIterations; i += 1) {
    const latestTarget = findRenderedModelElement(bpmnId) || target;
    const targetRect = latestTarget.getBoundingClientRect?.();
    const viewportRect = viewport.getBoundingClientRect?.();

    const anchor = getNavigationAnchorFractions(bpmnId);
    const desiredLeft = viewportRect.left + viewportRect.width * anchor.x;
    const desiredTop = viewportRect.top + viewportRect.height * anchor.y;

    const dx = targetRect.left - desiredLeft;
    const dy = targetRect.top - desiredTop;

    const needsX = Math.abs(dx) > 90;
    const needsY = Math.abs(dy) > 70;
    if (!needsX && !needsY) break;

    if (needsX) {
      clickBestPanButton(
        latestTarget,
        Math.abs(dx),
        dx > 0 ? controls.right : controls.left,
        dx > 0 ? controls.left : controls.right,
        "x",
        anchor
      );
    }

    if (needsY) {
      clickBestPanButton(
        latestTarget,
        Math.abs(dy),
        dy > 0 ? controls.down : controls.up,
        dy > 0 ? controls.up : controls.down,
        "y",
        anchor
      );
    }
  }
}

But it also checks if pan is inverted via clickBestPanButton()

Spoiler
/**
 * Chooses and clicks the most effective Galilei pan button for one axis.
 */
function clickBestPanButton(target, previousDistance, primaryButton, fallbackButton, axis, anchor = { x: 0.45, y: 0.45 }) {
  const beforeRect = target.getBoundingClientRect?.();
  clickGalileiPanButton(primaryButton);
  const afterRect = target.getBoundingClientRect?.();
  if (!isUsableRect(beforeRect) || !isUsableRect(afterRect)) return;

  // If the target did not move or moved clearly in the wrong direction, undo
  // with the opposite control and use that opposite control once. This keeps
  // the function robust if SAP's tooltip wording is interpreted differently.
  const moved = axis === "x" ? afterRect.left - beforeRect.left : afterRect.top - beforeRect.top;
  if (Math.abs(moved) < 0.5) return;

  // The caller passes previousDistance only as a guard against clearly bad
  // moves. Recompute against the viewport anchor for a reliable comparison.
  const viewport = findGalileiViewport(target);
  const viewportRect = viewport?.getBoundingClientRect?.();
  const newRect = target.getBoundingClientRect?.();
  if (!isUsableRect(viewportRect) || !isUsableRect(newRect)) return;
  const desired = axis === "x"
    ? viewportRect.left + viewportRect.width * anchor.x
    : viewportRect.top + viewportRect.height * anchor.y;
  const newDistance = Math.abs((axis === "x" ? newRect.left : newRect.top) - desired);

  if (newDistance > previousDistance + 20 && fallbackButton) {
    clickGalileiPanButton(fallbackButton);
    clickGalileiPanButton(fallbackButton);
  }
}



PS:

It's already passed the point of TL;DR , so pretty short:

Hope those extensions would help at least someone.

Of course, I do not collect any data with it. You can always check source code by provided links and by "%LOCALAPPDATA%\Google\Chrome\User Data\Default\Extensions" path.

AI is obviously unbelievably strong helper but still, unfortunately, not perfect, "Where used" extension was written within two chill hours at the weekend watching football. "Colorizer and Panel" took quite a lot of time because of constant AI attempts to standardize, ignore problems and breaking what was working to solve non-working stuff 😁.  But, in any case, it saves hours and hours of work.


If you have any questions or encountered some bugs (it should be there 🤣) always welcome to write there in the comments, codeberg, email and etc.

entry.title: Re: Entering the New Era of Agentic AI for ABAP Development
entry.content:
Hi @lienards ,

I’m really excited to see the direction SAP is taking with AI and developer tooling. This all sounds very promising and I’m looking forward to seeing how it evolves over time.

I still have a few questions and also some feedback:

  • Is Joule for Developers fundamentally different from SAP-ABAP-1? So far, SAP-ABAP-1 still feels very limited, and the product page even mentions that it is not recommended yet for productive code generation use cases. That’s honestly a bit unfortunate, because most other large AI models also struggle with ABAP as they don't have knowledge about DDIC and available SAP dev.objects, so I have my hopes high for SAP’s own solution here.
  • What is the first target scope of ADT for VS Code besides the AI features? Will it support development same classic ABAP development object types as Eclipse which do not exist in BTP ABAP env.?
  • Are there any plans to make parts of the tooling open source so we can contribute to it? 🙏
  • Is there a roadmap or a beta or early adopter program that customers or partners can join for either of these topics?
  • The consumption-based pricing model for Joule for Developers is really great news. Do you know whether something similar is planned for Joule for Consultants? The current flat-fee per user model is the biggest reason why we do not roll it out to a broader audience beyond power users.

Really looking forward to hearing more from you. Kudos to you and your team!

entry.title: Your Foundation for Continuous Success with SAP SuccessFactors
entry.content:
Every SAP SuccessFactors customer starts from the same place: the Foundational Success Plan.

Included with every SAP cloud solution, the Foundational Success Plan exists to do something essential but often underestimated: keep your HR landscape stable, supported, and evolving as SAP SuccessFactors continues to change and improve. It is the baseline that ensures your organisation can run reliably today while staying informed and prepared for what comes next.

For many customers, this foundation is what makes cloud HR sustainable in the first place.

It is easy to overlook the Foundational Success Plan precisely because it is always there. But for most customers, it is what makes continuous change manageable rather than disruptive, predictable rather than reactive, and sustainable rather than exhausting.

What the Foundational Success Plan Is Designed to Do

The Foundational Success Plan exists to ensure that your SAP SuccessFactors environment can run reliably while it evolves continuously - Cloud HR does not stand still.

SAP SuccessFactors receives regular updates, enhancements, and innovations, and customers must absorb this change while maintaining day‑to‑day business continuity. The role of the Foundational Success Plan is to create the conditions where that balance is possible.

It does this by providing a consistent layer of guidance, operational support, and preventive safeguards that are included as standard with every SAP cloud subscription. The focus is not acceleration or optimisation, but stability through ongoing change.

Guided Transformation as a Starting Point

 Even before go‑live, the Foundational Success Plan plays a role through guided transformation supported by SAP Cloud ALM and Onboarding. This ensures that implementations, upgrades, and transitions follow a structured and proven methodology rather than relying on ad‑hoc approaches.

For customers, this translates into more predictable outcomes, clearer governance, and fewer surprises as projects move from planning into delivery. It establishes a common language and structure for change that continues beyond the initial implementation phase.

Staying Informed and Oriented as the Solution Evolves

Once live, customers are not expected to “figure things out alone.”

The Foundational Success Plan gives access to self‑service enablement, and advisory content, helping HR and IT teams stay informed about new features, changes, and best practices as the solution continues to evolve. This includes documentation, learning resources, and product information that support informed adoption over time.

This layer is particularly important for organisations that prioritise controlled adoption: understanding what is changing, what matters to them, and when action is required.

Protecting Business Continuity in the Cloud

At its core, the Foundational Success Plan is about protecting the operational health of your HR landscape.

That includes technical operations for cloud, which help ensure that the environment remains stable, available, and performant, and preventive mission‑critical support with defined SLAs, designed to identify and address potential issues before they impact the business.

For many customers, this is the most visible aspect of the Foundational Success Plan - not because issues occur frequently, but because when they do, there is already a structured support model in place.

Why the Foundation Matters Even When Nothing Is “Wrong”

The value of the Foundational Success Plan is often most evident when things are running smoothly.

It provides predictability in total cost of ownership, clarity in how support is delivered, and confidence that SAP is continuously engaged in supporting your success - not just at go‑live, but throughout the lifecycle of your cloud solutions.

For organisations with stable operations and incremental change, this foundation is often exactly what they need.

When Customers Begin to Look Beyond the Foundation

As organisations mature with SAP SuccessFactors, the nature of their questions often evolves.

Instead of focusing primarily on keeping systems running, customers begin to ask how to adopt innovation faster, how to optimise processes proactively, how to extract more value from new capabilities, especially AI, and how to do so without increasing risk or complexity.

These questions do not replace the need for a strong foundation; they build on it.

Extending the Foundation with Advanced and Max Success Plans

This is where Advanced and Max Success Plans come into the picture. Both plans are subscriptions that build on top of the Foundational Success Plan, extending its capabilities rather than replacing it:

  • The Advanced Success Plan adds proactive innovation and adoption guidance, activation and optimisation services, AI‑driven quality insights, and a more hands‑on engagement model for customers who want to accelerate value and continuously improve how they use SAP SuccessFactors.
  • The Max Success Plan goes further by assisting with more complex situations, and by introducing a strategic partnership model with dedicated expertise, transformation safeguarding, and leadership level oversight for organisations operating at scale or undergoing complex, multi year HR transformations.

Each plan assumes the Foundational Success Plan as its baseline and responds to increasing ambition, complexity, and pace.

Start with the Foundation, Grow When You’re Ready

 Every SAP SuccessFactors customer benefits from the Foundational Success Plan. It ensures that your HR environment can operate reliably while accommodating continuous change, without additional contracts or decisions.

As your organisation evolves, SAP gives you the flexibility to extend that foundation deliberately, choosing more advanced engagement models only when your needs call for them.

The right next step is rarely about choosing a plan. It is about understanding what level of guidance and partnership best supports where your organisation is today, and where it wants to go next. Your Customer Success Manager or Account Executive can help explore that conversation, starting from the foundation you already have.

entry.title: Migrating to Cloud HCM: Insights from Cameco’s Success (Americas)
entry.content:
Turning HR process migration into business momentum

The desire for better employee experiences and efficiency gains is making HR innovation a priority for many organizations. However, navigating the path from on-premises solutions to AI-enabled, cloud human capital management (HCM) solutions can seem risky and overwhelming.

Join us for a webinar featuring Cameco, one of the largest global uranium fuel providers, as they share highlights of their migration to SAP SuccessFactors HCM. They’ll revisit lessons learned and look ahead to what’s next in their continued HR evolution. You’ll leave the session with:

  • Practical insights into planning and executing an HCM migration
  • A business case for making the move, and insider knowledge of what it takes to drive success
  • Recommendations and guidance for avoiding risk and gaining traction throughout the transformation

Gather the insight to accelerate your own transition to better experiences and greater business value.

Register now

entry.title: Combined Production Orders in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
Optimizing Production Efficiency Through Shared Operations

Manufacturing industries such as steel, paper, textile, tools, and special-purpose machinery often face a common challenge: multiple products or production orders require identical manufacturing operations. Running these operations separately can increase setup time, machine downtime, and production costs.

To address this challenge functionality of Combined Production Orders enables manufacturers to consolidate identical operations or even entire production orders into a single executable production process.

This blog explains the concept of Combined Production Orders, business benefits, prerequisites, process flow, key constraints and important operation considerations.

 

What is a Combined Production Order?

A Combined Production Order allows manufacturers to combine entire production orders or selected sequences of common operations from multiple original production orders into one consolidated production order.

The combined order becomes the execution order on the shop floor, while costs and production quantities are ultimately distributed back to the original production orders.

The functionality is particularly useful in industries where multiple products share the same processing steps, such as: Melting, Casting, Rolling, Heat Treatment, Dyeing, Cutting etc.

Instead of repeatedly setting up machines for same operation, manufacturers can execute multiple production orders together in one efficient production run.

 

Business Benefits of Combined Production Orders

1. Reduces Setup and Changeover Time

Combining orders reduces the number of machine setup changes required between production runs.  This results in lower setup labor costs, reduced machine downtime and higher production throughput.

2. Better Machine Utilization

Machines spend more time producing and less time idle during changeovers. This results in improved overall equipment effectiveness (OEE), higher capacity utilization, reduced non-productive time.

3. Improved Product Consistency

Executing similar operations together can reduce process variability. This results in improved product quality, more stable production conditions, and reduced operational variation.

 

Combined Production Order vs. Collective Production Order

These two functionalities are supported in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, but it is important to highlight the differences between the two as they serve very different purposes.

 

Aspect

Combined Production Order

Collective Production Order

Purpose

Consolidates similar operations

Links multi-level manufacturing orders

Focus

Production execution efficiency

BOM-driven dependent production

Typical Use Case

Shared machine operations

Direct production of subassemblies

Warehouse Posting

Finished goods posted separately to original orders

Subassemblies may bypass warehouse

Relationship Between Orders

Independent orders combined operationally

Orders linked across BOM hierarchy

Thus, the Key differences are:

  • Collective Orders are used when subassemblies are produced directly for superior orders without intermediate warehousing
  • Combined Orders are used to optimize production execution by consolidating common operations.

 

Key Prerequisites for Combined Production Orders

Note: This is not an exhaustive list. Always refer to SAP Help documentation for the complete set of prerequisites and restrictions.

1. Scope Item 7TO Must Be Activated

The scope item: 7TO – Production Order Combination must be activated in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

Quick Validation: In the Manage Production Orders app, verify that the Combine Order action is available.

2. Materials Must Support In-House Production

In the Material Master (MRP 2 view), procurement type must be:

  • E- In-House Production, or
  • X- Both Procurement Types

Combined Production Orders are only applicable for in-house manufacturing.

3. Orders Must Use Event-Based Costing

Combined Production Orders require event-based WIP and variance posting.

4. Unit of Measure Compatibility

The unit of measure across orders must either be identical or be convertible using alternative units of measure

 

Two Ways to Combine Production Orders

System provides two approaches to combine orders

1. Combining Entire Orders

Multiple production orders with identical operation sequences are merged into one combined production order. Typically used when the entire production flow is common across products.

To combine complete production orders, the following conditions must be met:

  • Orders must belong to the same plant
  • Orders must contain material numbers at header level
  • Production Order must be in Created status

2. Combining Selected Operations

Only specific common operations across production orders are combined. Typically, useful when only a portion of the manufacturing process is shared

To combine operations from different orders:

  • Operations must belong to the same plant
  • Operations must use the same work center
  • Operations must use identical control keys
  • Operation sequences must match
  • No gaps allowed in operation numbering i.e. one cannot combine operations 0010 and 0030 if operation 0020 exists between them.

 

Configuration Requirements

  • Production Order Type

SSCUI 102004 – Define Production Order Types

Verify order type YBOC – Production Order Processing Combination Order is available. This order type is delivered by SAP Best Practices for S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

 

  • RA (Result Analysis) Key and EB (Event Based) Processing Key for Order Type

SSCUI 102431 – Define Order Type Dependent Parameters

For Plant + Order Type YBOC ensure the Cost Accounting Settings:

  • RA Key/ EB Processing Key = RSEWB
  • Distribution Rule =
    • PP5 – Production Full Settlement, or
    • PP6 – Production Full Settlement (GR Quantity)

These settings enable event-based WIP and variance posting.

 

End-to-End Process Flow

This section covers end to end process flow from supply chain and manufacturing perspective, highlighting key elements in each step.

Step 1 – Create Standard Production Orders

Standard Production Orders (Order Type – YBM1: Make To Stock Production Order) are created manually or via conversion of Planning Proposals

Example Scenario

Order

Quantity

Planned End Date

1000862

10 PC

05-June

1000863

15 PC

12-June

Order Information

Order#: 1000862

Order #: 1000863

 

Input Components

Order#: 1000862

In this example 1 unit each of raw material components is used for producing 1 PC of output material. Thus, 10 units of each raw material is used to produce 10 pc of output product.

 

Order #: 1000863

Similarly in this example, 1 unit each of raw material components is used for producing 1 PC of output material. Thus, 15 units of each raw material is used to produce 15 pc of output product.

 

Operations & Processing Time

Order

Setup Time

Processing Time

1000862

30 min

10 min

1000863

30 min

15 min

Order#: 1000862

In this example input materials go through four operations to make the output product. It takes setup time of 30 mins and processing time of 1 min/ unit produced, thus the processing time of 10 mins per operation.

Order#: 1000863

Similarly, in this example input materials go through four operations to make the output product. It takes setup time of 30 mins and processing time of 1 min/ unit produced, thus the processing time of 15 mins per operation.

 

Step 2 – Create Combined Production Order

This step does the following:

  • Creates a new combined order (Type: YBOC)
  • Copies eligible operations into the combined order
  • Deactivates those operations in the original orders
  • Removes their capacity requirements from the original orders
  • Transfers component reservations into the combined order
  • The original orders still exist and remain traceable

The action to Combine orders is performed in the app Manage Production Orders.

Select the eligible production orders -> Choose Combine Order -> Select Create Combined Order or Create Combined Operations -> Choose a leading/ reference order -> Combined order created

In this example we are combining the complete order via Create Combined Order action

Select eligible production orders & choose combine order action

Select standard orders 1000862 and 1000863 and choose Combine Order -> Create Combined Order

 

Choose a leading/ reference order

Leading/ reference order is used for defaulting values in the combined order.

Here 1000862 is selected as the leading order

 

Combined Order is created

The outcome of the above action is the creation of Combined Order. In this example combined order 2000182 is created. Note the different number range for combined order and also the Combination Indicator = "Combined by Order (H)"

Quantity and Planned End Date

Quantity of the combined order is 25 EA (sum of the quantity from the original orders)

Planned End Date: 5th June (taken from leading order and scheduled backwards)

Order Items

Order Items section shows the original orders combined to create combined order.

Original orders 1000862 and 1000863 combined

Input Components

Component quantities are aggregated from original order and requirement dates are rescheduled based on the combined order.

 

Operations and Processing Time

At operation level, setup time is taken from the leading order (i.e. take once from the leading order) and the processing time is the sum of individual orders.

In this example Setup Time is 30 mins and processing time is 25 mins

Step 3 – Stage Materials for Production

Material staging for production is performed via Stage Materials for Production app. It is performed against the combined production order, and not the original order.

Step 4 – Release Production Order

Release is performed on the combined order. When the combined order is released, original production orders are automatically released.

 

Step 5 – Pick Components for Production Order

Component picking is executed with reference to the combined order. This is typically done using Pick Components for Production Order app.

 

Step 6 – Confirm Production Operations

Production confirmations are primarily executed at the combined order level.

Apps Used

 

App

Usage

Confirm Production Operation

Used for all operations except the last

Confirm Production Order Operation

Used for the last operation and quantity distribution

Confirm Production Operation is a Fiori app and is used for confirming the Yield and the Setup and Processing time.

Confirm Production Order Operation is a GUI app and is used for confirming Yield, Setup & Processing time and Quantity Distribution.

 

Quantity Distribution is required to distribute combined order yield to the original orders. This ensures accurate inventory assignment, variance allocation and order-level traceability.

When the yield quantity is equal to the combined order total quantity, quantity distribution automatically happens but still needs to be confirmed. Whan the yield quantity is different from the combined order total quantity; distribution must be done manually.

 

Step 7 – Post Goods Receipt

Goods Receipt is posted against the individual original production orders, not the combined order, as combined order is primarily an execution-oriented object rather than a financial ownership object. Thus, inventory needs to be traced to individual orders for batch and serial number traceability. Further, for accurate product costing, WIP settlement, Variance calculation and financial traceability need to happen at the individual order level.

Goods Receipt is done using Post Goods Receipt for Production Order app.

Goods Receipt: Order #: 1000862

Goods Receipt: Order #: 1000863

Allowed changes after Combined Order created

One of the common questions comes regarding what can be changed in Combined Order and Original Order once the Combined Order is created. This section highlights what changes are possible in each order.

Changes Allowed in Combined Order

While the combined order is in Created status:

Change Type

Allowed?

Change Dates

Yes

Change Quantities

No

Change Combined Operations

Generally, No

Change Components

Yes

Add Original Orders

Yes

Remove Original Orders

Yes (minimum two orders must remain)

Cancel Combined Order

Yes

 Changes Allowed in Original Order

Change Type

Allowed?

Change Dates

Yes

Change Quantities

Yes

Change Non-Combined Operations

Yes

Change Combined Operations

No

Change Non-Combined Components

Yes

Combined operations become deactivated in the original orders and are controlled through the combined order.

 

Product Costing in Combined Order scenario

Understanding product costing is critical when implementing Combined Production Orders. A Combined Production Order is primarily used for operation execution optimization. Therefore, following considerations are critical:

  • Costs collected at the combined order level must ultimately be distributed back to the original production orders
  • Proper settlement logic is essential
  • Event-based costing configuration becomes extremely important

Incorrect costing configuration can lead to:

  • Incorrect WIP calculation
  • Improper variance allocation
  • Financial reconciliation issues

A deeper discussion on costing and settlement behavior deserves a dedicated topic of its own and would be covered in a separate blog.

 

Summary

Combined Production Orders in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition provide manufacturers with a powerful capability to optimize production execution where multiple orders share common operations.

By reducing setup time, improving machine utilization, and streamlining operational execution, organizations can achieve significant manufacturing efficiency gains.

However, successful implementation requires careful attention to:

  • Operational prerequisites
  • Costing configuration
  • Order eligibility rules
  • Yield distribution logic
  • Financial settlement behavior

When implemented correctly, Combined Production Orders can significantly improve shop floor efficiency while maintaining accurate order-level traceability and costing.

 

Reference Material

SAP Help Document: Combining Production Orders

entry.title: Why SAP’s Autonomous Enterprise Strategy Matters More Than You Think
entry.content:
At SAP Sapphire 2026, it became clear that SAP is not just evolving ERP. It's rethinking how enterprises operate. The Autonomous Enterprise is more than a new label. It reflects a shift toward systems that can actively drive outcomes using agentic AI. It changes how enterprise landscapes are designed, governed, and run.

SAP's Evolution Towards Agentic AI 

From an architectural standpoint, SAP’s journey reflects a deliberate and well‑orchestrated evolution—from foundational systems to intelligence, and now toward autonomy:

  • R/3 era: Built the foundation with client-server architecture
  • ECC era: Expanded into a digital core, integrating end-to-end business processes
  • S/4HANA era: Transformed performance with in-memory computing (HANA)
  • Today: Moving to cloud operations powered by data and AI, unifying applications and insights
  • Next: Evolving into the Autonomous Enterprise, where agentic AI systems help run business processes

The foundation is already in place. In most organizations, ERP remains the most valuable source of data that AI and agents can use to drive better decisions.

However, moving from cloud ERP to a more agent-driven ERP is not a simple shift. It requires the right foundation across data, development, and governance. This is exactly where SAP is focusing.

From Complex Deployments To Simple Strategy 

If you’ve moved from ECC to S/4HANA, you’re on the right path. If you’ve taken the next step and moved S/4HANA to the cloud, you’re already ahead of many organizations. And if you’re layering in the tools and capabilities needed to bring AI into your ERP landscape, you’re not just keeping up—you’re positioning your enterprise for what’s next.

What SAP is doing now is bringing together capabilities that were previously spread across different areas:

  • BTP (Business Technology Platform): the foundation for apps, integration, and AI
  • Business Data Cloud (BDC): a unified data layer connecting SAP and non-SAP data
  • All combined into one: The SAP Business AI Platform 

This platform connects tools, applications, processes, and data into one cohesive environment. In simple terms, SAP is moving away from a collection of separate tools toward a single platform that can support both today’s needs and the future vision of autonomous operations.

SAP Business AI Platform introduces a simplified, strategic architecture built around three core layers—build, context, and governance—designed to make agentic AI scalable, reliable, and enterprise-ready. 

With the Business AI Platform, organizations now have everything they need to build agents. The next step is understanding how those agents actually come together to get work done in an agent-driven environment.

The Autonomous Execution 

From a user perspective, this is designed to feel simple and intuitive. A business user works through Joule Work using natural language, asking questions or triggering actions, while the complexity of agentic AI stays behind the scenes.

Users get clear insights, recommendations, and outcomes, without having to manage processes, systems, or integrations. The experience is focused on getting work done, while the orchestration and execution are handled invisibly in the background.

In the example above, the Sourcing Manager interacts with Joule Work at the top, which acts as the entry point. Behind that, a Sourcing Assistant coordinates multiple domain agents across finance, procurement, customer experience, and HR.

Each of these agents is tied to a specific Joule persona and executes predefined scenarios such as creating a purchase requisition or detecting a supply chain delay. These scenarios access enterprise data from different domains like finance, spend, supply chain, HR, and customer experience.

The agents collaborate, retrieve data, and execute workflows across systems, but all of that activity remains abstracted from the user, who only sees the final outcome and any relevant information needed to make decisions.

The Key Takeaways

  • SAP Autonomous Enterprise is the evolution from Cloud ERP to Agentic ERP
  • SAP Business AI Platform is the platform for building, contextualizing, and governing AI
  • SAP isn't just layering AI on ERP they are reimagining the future of how work is done
  • Organizations that have adopted SAP S/4HANA and Cloud are well positioned 

SAP Customer Advisory

If your organization is planning or evaluating an SAP strategy, our team at SAP Customer Advisory can help. We offer tailored guidance to align your architecture vision with business goals, accelerate transformation, and maximize value creation.

You can reach out to me directly to explore how SAP can help shape your architecture vision and drive successful outcomes — connect with me on LinkedIn.

entry.title: Re: Accept/Revoke MRP Run results in the Stock/Requirements List and MRP Fiori Apps
entry.content:
@Align779 I am not able to vote but would like to.

Some thoughts:

My background is primarily in heavy industry (mining, oil & gas, utilities, etc.) where MRP is used to generate requisitions for indirect materials (MRO).  When we run MRP it is usually covering ten's of thousands to hundred's of thousands of materials at a time, so accepting / revoking the results is not practical.

MD04 has (and is) the standard used by everyone (all roles) on site to view material availability, master data, open orders, etc. This is NOT just a planner's screen.  We are already struggling with the loss of MD06 (the MRP List) since managing by exception was key to managing this many materials on a daily basis.

entry.title: Learning by Playing: How ERPsim Turns SAP Courses into Immersive, Real‑World Labs by Prof. Andrea
entry.content:
Last week I had the opportunity to deliver the hands-on training “Learning by Playing: The Power of SAP ERPsim Simulation in the Classroom,” a session focused on the use of business simulation environments for teaching integrated processes in SAP.
 
The experience once again highlighted ERPsim’s tremendous potential as an experiential learning tool, enabling the complexity of business management to be brought into a dynamic, competitive, and highly interactive environment.
 
ERPsim transforms traditional SAP learning into an immersive experience where participants not only execute transactions but actually run a company in real time within a fully integrated ERP environment.
 
During the simulation, teams must make business decisions continuously and in a coordinated way: demand analysis and market behavior, production planning and MRP execution, purchasing and inventory management, pricing strategy, and the interpretation of KPIs and operational and financial reports.
 
One of the most valuable aspects of the session was seeing how quickly participants grasp the cross-functional nature of business processes in SAP. Decisions taken in one area have an immediate impact across the rest of the organization: sales forecasting shapes MRP; MRP generates procurement and production requirements; production capacity affects product availability; and ultimately all of this impacts sales, margins, and profitability.
 
The simulation allows SAP to be seen as an integrated ecosystem of processes, rather than as an isolated set of functional transactions.
 
In addition, ERPsim introduces a particularly relevant component from a training perspective: data-driven decision-making. Dashboards and reports cease to be merely informative elements and become strategic tools that enable teams to react to the market, identify trends, and adjust operations in real time.
 
Another differentiating element is the development of transferable, cross-functional competencies. The simulation dynamic requires coordination across roles, continuous communication, shared leadership, and decision-making under time pressure—mirroring situations commonly found in real business environments.
 
Here, gamification is not an add-on, but a mechanism that significantly increases participants’ engagement, motivation, and commitment, fostering a much more active and meaningful learning experience.
 
As a member of the academic ecosystem linked to SAP University Alliances and of the SAP Academic Board Iberoamerica, it is especially encouraging to see how these methodologies continue to gain traction as an effective way to bring ERP systems into the classroom from a practical, strategic, and integrative perspective.
 
Without a doubt, ERPsim represents an excellent opportunity to evolve SAP education toward more applied, collaborative models oriented toward real-world business decision-making.
 
I would be delighted to continue sharing experiences and best practices with any institution or instructor interested in incorporating this type of simulation into their programs.
 
Comment from the SAP Academic Partnerships team: We really want to thank Prof. Andrea Ollo enormously for this exclusive session for professors, as an initiative of being part in the SAP Academic Board Iberoamerican (ABI) 2025-2026 from the academic community to the academic community!  For more details about the ABI kindly check out: Welcome to the SAP Academic Board Iberoamerica mem... - SAP Community
 
(Find here below the original text in Spanish from Prof. Andrea Ollo, UPNA University, Spain) 

La semana pasada tuve la oportunidad de impartir la formación práctica “Aprender Jugando: El Poder de la Simulación SAP ERPsim en el Aula”, una sesión centrada en el uso de entornos de simulación empresarial para la enseñanza de procesos integrados en SAP.

La experiencia volvió a poner de manifiesto el enorme potencial de ERPsim como herramienta de aprendizaje experiencial, permitiendo trasladar la complejidad de la gestión empresarial a un entorno dinámico, competitivo y altamente interactivo.

ERPsim transforma el aprendizaje tradicional de SAP en una experiencia inmersiva donde las y los participantes no solo ejecutan transacciones, sino que gestionan una empresa en tiempo real dentro de un entorno ERP completamente integrado.

Durante la simulación, los equipos deben tomar decisiones de negocio de manera continua y coordinada: análisis de demanda y comportamiento del mercado, planificación de producción y ejecución de MRP, gestión de compras e inventarios, definición de estrategias de precios, interpretación de KPIs e informes operativos y financieros.

Uno de los aspectos más valiosos de la sesión fue observar cómo las y los participantes comprenden rápidamente la naturaleza transversal de los procesos empresariales en SAP. Las decisiones tomadas en un área tienen un impacto inmediato en el resto de la organización: la previsión de ventas condiciona el MRP, el MRP genera necesidades de aprovisionamiento y producción, la capacidad productiva afecta a la disponibilidad de producto y, finalmente, todo ello repercute en ventas, márgenes y rentabilidad.

La simulación permite visualizar SAP como un ecosistema integrado de procesos y no como un conjunto aislado de transacciones funcionales.

Además, ERPsim introduce un componente especialmente relevante desde el punto de vista formativo: la toma de decisiones basada en datos. Los cuadros de mando y reportes que se pueden realizar dejan de ser elementos meramente informativos para convertirse en herramientas estratégicas que permiten reaccionar al mercado, identificar tendencias y ajustar operaciones en tiempo real.

Otro elemento diferencial es el desarrollo de competencias transversales. La dinámica de simulación exige coordinación entre roles, comunicación continua, liderazgo compartido y toma de decisiones bajo presión temporal, reproduciendo situaciones muy próximas a las que se encuentran en entornos empresariales reales.

La gamificación actúa aquí no como un elemento accesorio, sino como un mecanismo que incrementa significativamente el nivel de implicación, motivación y compromiso de las y los participantes, favoreciendo un aprendizaje mucho más activo y significativo.

Como miembro del ecosistema académico vinculado a SAP University Alliances y miembro del Academic Board Iberoamerica SAP, resulta especialmente positivo comprobar cómo este tipo de metodologías continúan consolidándose como una vía efectiva para acercar los sistemas ERP al aula desde una perspectiva práctica, estratégica e integradora.

Sin duda, ERPsim representa una excelente oportunidad para evolucionar la enseñanza de SAP hacia modelos más aplicados, colaborativos y orientados a la toma de decisiones empresariales reales.

Estaré encantada de seguir compartiendo experiencias y buenas prácticas con cualquier institución o docente interesada/o en incorporar este tipo de simulaciones en sus programas formativos.

entry.title: Re: Building Agentic Hybrid RAG with Knowledge Graphs + Vectors - Developer Guide
entry.content:
Hi Zomono, Thanks for your comments.

A KG only contains what you explicitly modeled in ontology. Anything narrative, procedural, or outside your ontology — it won't find. Vector RAG catches the rest. Property Graph is easier to store in single table and retrieve multi-hop traversal because of SQL based and its faster than RDF/SPARQL. But you lose formal ontology reasoning.

hope this answers your question.

entry.title: Introduction - Liilet Sanders SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Implementation Certification
entry.content:
My name is Liilet Sanders, an Enterprise Project Manager and Certified Scrum Master based in Metro Atlanta, Georgia. I got into this work through an unconventional path, starting in Accounting then moved to a Financial Systems Analyst doing SAP functional work across FICO, MM, SD, and Concur, then eventually into Enterprise Project Management where I've spent 10+ years delivering complex SaaS implementations and ERP transformations across Healthcare, FinTech, and Auto Warranty.

I'm currently pursuing my SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Implementation Certification, building on completed credentials in AWS Generative AI, Cloud 101, and SAP Support Accreditation. My interests live at the intersection of SAP functional delivery and enterprise project execution particularly how S/4HANA Cloud is reshaping implementation methodology, fit-to-standard design, and AI-driven process automation.

What makes my background unusual is the combination of hands-on SAP functional knowledge and enterprise-scale Project Management discipline most people have one, not both. I don't just manage SAP projects. I understand what's being built inside them.

I'm looking to connect with SAP S/4HANA SME’s, hiring managers, mentors, and fellow practitioners especially those implementing S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition in the Atlanta area or across the U.S. based organizations. If you're serious about building in the SAP ecosystem, let's connect!

entry.title: Re: How to change company address for my customer number
entry.content:
If a customer's address data needs to be changed or updated, contact the SAP Account Executive and include the information below. If you do not know who your SAP Account Executive is, refer to KBA 2626344.

  • Customer Number
  • Customer Name
  • Old Address
  • New Address
  • Letterhead showing updated address (if available)
  • Indicate if the change is for the main address for the company or if an alternate address should be listed

The SAP Account Executive will do what is necessary to change the customer's address with SAP Order Management (contractual team).

Reference KBA: 2602869 - How to change the company address for your customer number - SAP for Me - SAP for Me

entry.title: How to restrict the policies being applied to specified customer network
entry.content:
Hi SAP,

Are in the middle of a migration to SAP Rise, and we have systems both on-prem and in Rise. As systems in Rise are managed differently we need to have checks that apply only to on-prem and some that only apply to Rise.

Our idea is that we us the this flag in the check: system_attributes="customer_network:XYZ" but what is the exact name we should use here? customer_network seems not to be correct and we cannot find any documentation for this.

Please advise what to use and/or if you have any other ide how to differentiate between systems in rise/on-prem in the checks

 

Br

PERSON_NAME


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: How to restrict the policies being applied to specifi...
entry.content:
Hello customer,

The customer network can be filtered for the policy by adding the system attributes in the policy. Please find the sample syntax below, and kindly update the system attributes with the customer network in the policy. The validation will then be performed only for the specified customer network.

<checkitem desc=" dynp/confirmskip1screen " id="01" system_roles="DEV:TEST:QA:PROD:SANDBOX” system_attributes="LMDB_CUST_NET:SAP_Network">

For the other customer network, replace the value 'SAP_Network'.

For more details about the system_attributes, please refer to SAP Focused Run Expert Portal under Checkitem Attributes -> system_attributes section.

Best regards,

SAP Support
