entry.title: Convergent Invoicing SSCUIs not visible in Configuration - Scope Item activation required?
entry.content:
Hi SAP Community,

I'm working on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition implementation for a manufacturing company (display manufacturing industry).

**Issue:**
The following Convergent Invoicing-related SSCUIs are included in our BDCQ but are NOT visible in Central Business Configuration:

- 103286: Define Residence Time for Billable Items
- 103288: Define Changeable Fields for Billable Items
- 103285: Define Selection Variants
- 103331: Define Grouping Variants for Billable Items when Printing
- 103333: Define Offsetting Categories
- 103334: Define Transfer Posting Procedure for Offsetting
- 103332: Define Billing Item Types
- 103458: Assign Subprocesses to Classes

**Questions:**

1. **Do these SSCUIs require a specific Scope Item to be activated?**
- If yes, which Scope Item(s)? (e.g., 5IK, 3L1, 500178, or others?)

2. **Is Convergent Invoicing primarily for subscription/usage-based billing, or can it be used for standard manufacturing scenarios?**
- Our understanding: Convergent Invoicing consolidates multiple billing sources (e.g., SD Billing + Service fees) into a single invoice.
- Use case in manufacturing: Hardware sales (SD) + Maintenance service fees (Billable Items) → Single invoice
- Is this understanding correct?

3. **When is Convergent Invoicing necessary vs. standard SD Billing sufficient?**
- Our scenario: Display manufacturing with potential maintenance service contracts
- Should we activate Convergent Invoicing, or can we handle this with standard SD Billing?

**Environment:**
- SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
- Starter System (Evaluation)

Any guidance would be greatly appreciated!

Best regards,
byd

entry.title: Re: New User for Subaccount - using a distribution list e...
entry.content: You have to create user in IAS, not in the subaccount. The user list in the subaccount is a shadow list, useful only for role collection assignment. Authentication, and credentials are managed in the connected IDP. Unless you have setup IAS with federation with corporate IDP,  credential management is in IAS.

entry.title: Credit Management SSCUIs with "1QM, BD6" scope reference - Required when only BD6 (Basic Credit Mana
entry.content:
Hi SAP Community,

We are implementing SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition for a manufacturing company. Our scope includes **BD6 (Basic Credit Management)** but **NOT 1QM (Advanced Credit Management)**.

However, several Credit Management SSCUIs in our BDCQ have **both "1QM, BD6"** listed in the Scope Reference column.

**Questions:**

## 1. Are these SSCUIs still applicable when only BD6 is active?

The following SSCUIs reference both 1QM and BD6:

| SSCUI | Name | Scope Ref |
|-------|------|-----------|
| 101508 | Assign Sales Area to Credit Segment | 1QM, BD6 |
| 101518 | Define Automatic Credit Control | 1QM, BD6 |
| 101850 | Define Customer Credit Group | 1QM, BD6 |
| 102623 | Define Checking Rules | 1QM, BD6 |
| 102570 | Define Credit Groups | 1QM, BD6 |

**Question:**
- Should we still configure these SSCUIs even though 1QM is out of scope?
- Or are some of these settings only relevant for 1QM (Advanced Credit Management)?

## 2. Impact of changing standard settings (BD6 only scenario)

If we modify the standard settings in these SSCUIs under BD6-only scenario:
- Will it impact sales order processing (e.g., automatic credit blocks)?
- Are there operational risks if settings are misconfigured?
- Should we keep standard settings as-is for initial go-live?

## 3. What is the difference between BD6 (Basic) and 1QM (Advanced) Credit Management?

We need to understand:
- **Functional differences** between BD6 and 1QM
- Which features are available in BD6 vs. 1QM only
- Specifically for the 5 SSCUIs listed above:
- Which settings are BD6-specific?
- Which settings are 1QM-specific?
- Which settings are shared?

For example:
- **Credit Segment (SSCUI 101508)**: Is this feature available in BD6, or only in 1QM?
- **Checking Rules (SSCUI 102623)**: Can we customize check rules in BD6, or is this 1QM-only?

## Environment
- **Product:** SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
- **System:** Starter System
- **Active Scope Items:** BD6 (Basic Credit Management)
- **Inactive Scope Items:** 1QM (Advanced Credit Management)
- **Industry:** Manufacturing

## Our Current Understanding (Please Confirm)

### Hypothesis 1: SSCUIs are shared between BD6 and 1QM
- These SSCUIs are used by both BD6 and 1QM
- Some fields/features may be disabled when only BD6 is active
- Standard settings work for BD6; customization is optional

### Hypothesis 2: Some settings are 1QM-only
- Credit Segment assignment (101508) might be 1QM-only
- BD6 uses simplified credit control without segments
- We should skip 1QM-specific settings

**Which hypothesis is correct?**

## Expected Response
1. Confirmation: Which of the 5 SSCUIs are required for BD6-only scenario
2. Functional difference: BD6 vs. 1QM feature comparison
3. Recommendation: Should we configure these SSCUIs or keep standard settings?

Thank you for your support!

Best regards,
[Your Name]

entry.title: Where the rubber meets the road - delivering value with next-gen SAP Ariba
entry.content:
We all nod along when we hear the newest terminology, but do we really know what it means? And do we know how to read through the noise when deciding which solutions to adopt? The tech world was already full of buzz words and acronyms, but AI has recently expanded our vocabulary tenfold. Gen AI, agentic, machine learning, deep learning, AI as a service… it goes on and on. As we hear about tools and technologies, companies are eager to differentiate themselves in this space, but many  forget that most of us are still on a steep learning curve. With the launch of next-gen SAP Ariba, I was curious to better understand what it means to be ‘AI native,’ so naturally, I asked AI for an analogy to explain ‘AI as a feature’ versus ‘AI native’ architecture. The results were insightful, and I continued to explore this analogy to relate it back to our next-gen SAP Ariba vision.

Think of ‘AI as a feature’ like the parking assist feature in your car - a super smart helper added to an existing automobile, designed to perform a singular task. Now, think about a completely autonomous self-driving vehicle. Everything about this car is designed around AI: sensors, maps, driving policies, safety systems, and operating controls. This is what it means to be ‘AI native’. This is now the architecture of next-gen SAP Ariba; AI will drive decisions end to end, throughout the entire source to pay process. Data, workflows, and decisions are built to learn and improve continuously, across the procurement suite.

While AI as a feature can definitely deliver quick wins, AI native architecture is the best option when decisions at scale drive value, automating processes end to end, or continuous improvement is needed to meet fast changing environments.

Taking the analogy a level deeper, self-driving cars operate through five main components. These systems leverage AI for real-time navigation, detection, and decision making.

  • Perception (Sensors & Vision): scans the environment to identify lane markings, traffic lights, pedestrians, and nearby vehicles
  • Sensor Fusion: combines the raw data from the sensors to track objects
  • Localization: determines the vehicle’s exact location, using GPS and high-definition maps to compare surroundings against known landmarks
  • Path Planning & Decision Making: algorithms and AI define the safest and most efficient path from one point to another, navigating traffic rules and obstacles
  • Control & Actuation: executes the planned path by controlling the vehicles acceleration, braking, and steering

Every component is designed to embed, engage, and operate with AI, with all systems working in tandem to replace a driver’s visual perception and physical control of the vehicle. Clean data, constant feedback, and seamlessly connected systems are needed to automate procurement processes as well. Knowing that AI only delivers value when it’s supported by the right data, processes, and architecture, SAP Ariba applications were rebuilt from the ground up. Next-gen SAP Ariba is now on SAP Business AI Platform, providing a unified real-time data foundation across the source-to-pay lifecycle. Just like the self-driving vehicle, SAP Ariba’s procurement suite is now designed entirely around AI and built on four main components.

  • Foundation on SAP Business AI Platform: the underlying technical framework of SAP applications, the platform for building, running, and governing AI
  • Joule (Conversational AI): an omnipresent, conversational AI copilot integrated across SAP applications, allowing users to retrieve insights and automate tasks with simple, natural language
  • Joule Agents (Automation Agents): intelligent agents that handle complex, end-to-end business workflows and interact with cross-application SAP data to complete tasks
  • Embedded AI (Built-in Intelligence): prebuilt AI features integrated directly into SAP applications (predictive forecasting, anomaly detection, automated invoice matching, etc.)

SAP Business AI Platform is the game changer here – this technical foundation delivers the modern, extensible platform and suite orchestration that can’t be achieved with AI as an add on feature. By making the move to SAP Business AI Platform, SAP Ariba can now support open API’s, cross-suite data consistency, and the responsiveness required for intelligent, AI-driven procurement operations.

Pun intended, Joule is now in the driver’s seat – reducing procurement friction by acting as an embedded, context-aware copilot across SAP Ariba, SAP S/4HANA, and SAP Business Network. Joule reduces cycle times, cuts exceptions, and lowers the number of human touches by turning natural language requests into actions, surfacing the right context at the right time, and then automating low-risk decisions – with guardrails of course. If you’re like me, you’re still a bit anxious to step into a fully autonomous car; it’s all good until it comes time to turn left in a busy intersection. SAP understands we are on this new journey together and we are keeping humans at the forefront. Our AI is here to boost, optimize, and enhance the user’s experience. Joule can sit in the driver’s seat, but you still have a steering wheel.

With the evolution of this technology alongside an era of constant disruption, only an AI-native platform can fully unlock the power of AI and agentic intelligence. To quote Baber Farooq, Senior Vice President of Product Marketing for SAP Ariba and SAP Fieldglass, “the launch of next-gen SAP Ariba is not an endpoint, it is the foundation for the future of procurement.”

While AI is revolutionizing procurement processes, one truth remains: the business outcomes that technology delivers are where the rubber meets the road.  When it comes to the cars (and procurement solutions) of the future, I’ll be looking for true, AI native builds. For more information, check out next-gen SAP Ariba on sap.com

entry.title: Visibility Is Already There: Stop Overengineering Your Yard in SAP EWM
entry.content:
Visibility Is Already There: Stop Overengineering Your Yard!

Manyat Thinking: Simplicity is the Ultimate Sophistication

Introduction

In many SAP EWM projects, teams often struggle with yard visibility. When a truck arrives, the first reaction is usually:  “We can’t see what’s inside the truck.” And from there, the solution becomes:
          . Create new storage types
          . Create yard bins
          .  Add additional Warehouse Tasks
          . Build extra tracking logic
But what if the visibility already exists in SAP EWM — and we are simply not using it correctly ?

The Trap of Overengineering

To solve visibility problems, projects often introduce:
                  . Logical yard bins for each truck
                   . Extra Warehouse Tasks only for visibility
                   . Additional stock movements for tracking
Initially, this feels like better control. However, over time it creates:
                    . More system load
                    . Slower warehouse execution
                    . Additional process complexity
                   .  Unnecessary maintenance effort
You start with a visibility problem and end up creating an execution problem.

The Manyat Approach: Visibility Already Exists

SAP EWM already provides a natural visibility chain:

Transportation Unit (TU) → Delivery → Material
This already tells us:
               . Which truck has arrived
               . Which deliveries belong to the truck
               . Which materials and quantities are expected
So the real question becomes:
If the information already exists, why are we recreating it through additional stock movements?

Using Existing Data for Smart Yard Planning

SAP EWM is already integrated with SAP TM and delivery processes. When a truck arrives:
        The Transportation Unit is linked to a Delivery
        The Delivery already contains material and quantity information
        Expected stock is already visible in the system

Using standard monitoring tools such as /SCWM/MON, teams can:
          . Track deliveries linked to a TU
         .  Identify expected material and quantities
         .  Match incoming stock with warehouse requirements
          . Prepare labor and resources in advance
        . Plan door assignments earlier
Planning can also be improved using:
          . Delivery priority
         . ETA and waiting time
         . Queue and sequencing logic
There is no need to create artificial stock just to “see” visibility.

A Shift in Thinking

Traditional thinking often looks like this:
Truck → Staging → Planning → Execution
But a smarter approach is:
Truck → Yard Planning → Staging → Execution
This small shift changes a lot. It allows warehouses to:
          . Reduce unnecessary warehouse movements
          . Improve planning accuracy
          . Reduce system load
         .  Execute operations faster
Most importantly, planning happens before execution bottlenecks appear.

Key Insight

For planning activities, expected data is often enough.
Actual confirmation should happen during execution — not during planning.
Creating additional warehouse movements only for visibility is not optimization.
It is overengineering.

Conclusion

A smart warehouse design is not about adding more configuration.
It is about understanding what already exists in SAP EWM and using it correctly.
The visibility was always there.
We just needed to look at it differently.

By following this visibility approach, warehouse teams can reduce confusion, avoid unnecessary movements, improve truck planning, and achieve better coordination between yard and warehouse operations.

Instead of recreating visibility again and again, the focus should be on using the existing visibility effectively for faster and more controlled execution.

How do you currently manage yard visibility and truck planning in your warehouse operations? I would love to hear different real-world approaches and experiences from the community.


— Manyat Thinking
Part of Manyat Project – SAP EWM Simplified By Rahul Kumar

entry.title: Re: BAPI_ENTRYSHEET_CREATE issue with service short text
entry.content:
Hi,

I found this note: https://me.sap.com/notes/1337342/E

It mentions a symptom related to the short text.
Even though it is not exactly what you are experiencing it did make me think that the usage of the short text field is somewhere configureable.
When a Service Entry Sheet(SES) is created using BAPI_ENTRYSHEET_CREATE with a service master record having "Short text change allowed(ASMD-CHGTEXT)" indicator set and if the short text field for the service is not filled in the BAPI then the system creates SES with service line short text as '$'.

I then looked further and apparently the CHGTEXT indicator can be configured via transaction ML02 based on the service type. 
As it turns out I don't have a system where this functionality is used and as such did not check it further.
Nonetheless it might give you some direction to look further into 🙂

If that is not it there is still the option that there is a badi or exit used in the flow that holds custom logic to overwrite the short text field. Use a trace or other manners of finding such potential points of customer adaptations in the flow.

Hope it helps.

Kr,
Joery

entry.title: Re: Part 1: Download Integration Packages to Google Drive
entry.content:
Thanks for the helpful blog! I noticed a small point in Step 11 regarding the Google Drive upload query parameter.

Currently, the query is:

path=/SAP/CPI/Iflow-Package/${property.packageName}&overwrite=true

Since the .zip extension is not included, the uploaded file in Google Drive may appear as an unknown file type.

You may want to update it to:

path=/SAP/CPI/Iflow-Package/${property.packageName}.zip&overwrite=true

This ensures the uploaded package is recognized correctly as a ZIP file in Google Drive.

entry.title: Collective order integration and child order consumption in SAP Digital Manufacturing
entry.content:
Introduction

In today’s manufacturing landscape, achieving seamless synchronization between ERP and shop-floor systems is critical for operational efficiency. One such advanced scenario is the handling of collective orders (parent–child relationships) and enabling automatic consumption of child orders within parent orders.

In this blog, I’ll walk you through a practical implementation of how SAP S/4HANA collective orders can be integrated with SAP Digital Manufacturing (DM) and how child order consumption can be automated during execution.

Business Scenario

In SAP S/4HANA, collective orders define a hierarchical relationship between production orders:

For example:

While S/4HANA inherently manages these relationships, SAP Digital Manufacturing does not directly interpret this hierarchy unless explicitly modeled.

Solution Overview

This solution addresses two key objectives:

  1. Establish parent–child relationships in SAP DM
  2. Automate consumption of child orders into parent orders upon completion

Step 1: Mapping Parent–Child Relationships in SAP DM

To replicate the hierarchy in SAP DM:

Create Custom Data Fields

Two custom fields were created in SAP DM:

  1. Parent Order
  2. Main Parent Order

These fields act as placeholders for hierarchy data coming from S/4HANA.

Data Transformation Using XSLT

Using XSLT transformation, values from S/4HANA were mapped to the custom fields during integration.

As a result:

  1. The order hierarchy becomes visible in SAP DM
  2. Data is accessible via the Custom Data tab in the Manage Order application

 

Step 2: Automating Child Order Consumption

Once hierarchy is established, the next step is to ensure the child order is automatically consumed when it is completed.

  • Production Process Design

A production process was developed with the following logic:

  1. Identify the parent order dynamically using the custom fields
  2. Trigger logic when a child order reaches completion
  3. Use standard SAP DM APIs to assemble the child order into the parent order

Step 3: Event-Based Automation Using PPD

To eliminate manual intervention:

  • The solution leverages Production Process Designer (PPD)
  • A Business Rule is configured with event type: Order Completed

This ensures that:

  • As soon as a child order is completed
  • The system automatically performs component consumption in the parent order

Validation & Results

To validate the implementation:

  • Use the SFC Report
  • Navigate to the Component Tab

Here, you can verify that:

  • The child order (1004077) is successfully assembled into the parent order (1004090)

Key Benefits

  • Seamless integration between SAP S/4HANA and SAP DM
  • Accurate representation of collective order hierarchy
  • Automated consumption reduces manual effort
  • Improved production traceability
  • Enhanced shop-floor execution efficiency

Conclusion

This approach demonstrates how SAP Digital Manufacturing can be extended to support advanced manufacturing scenarios like collective orders, bridging the gap between ERP planning and shop-floor execution.

By leveraging custom data fields, XSLT transformations, production processes, and event-driven automation, you can enable a scalable and intelligent manufacturing workflow.

Final Thoughts

Collective order handling is a powerful capability, especially in complex manufacturing environments with multi-level production. Extending this functionality into SAP DM unlocks real-time execution visibility and automation benefits.

If you find this useful or have implemented a similar scenario, feel free to share your thoughts!

 

Thanks,
Swanand Joshi

entry.title: Why does SAP allow MIRO posting for stock in Quality Inspection
entry.content:
In SAP, we observe that it is possible to post an invoice using MIRO even when the stock is still in Quality Inspection after Goods Receipt.

Business scenario:

  1. Purchase Order is created for a material.
  2. Goods Receipt (MIGO) is posted, and stock is posted to Quality Inspection stock due to QM integration.
  3. An inspection lot is created and Usage Decision (UD) is still pending.
  4. Before UD is completed, the vendor invoice is received.
  5. The invoice can still be posted successfully in MIRO.

We are aware that SAP provides QM-related procurement controls in Material Master such as:

  • QM Procurement Key
  • Active QM in Procurement 

After maintaining these settings, SAP applies a payment block to prevent vendor payment.

However, even with/without these settings, SAP still allows MIRO posting without issuing an error or warning related to the open inspection status.

Our questions:

  • Why does SAP standard behavior allow MIRO posting even when stock is still in Quality Inspection status?
  • If QM procurement controls exist (QM Procurement Key / QM in Procurement), why are they not enforced as a mandatory block or warning by default?
  • What is the design reasoning behind allowing invoice verification while quality inspection is still open?
  • In real business scenarios, what is the recommended best practice — allow invoice posting before Usage Decision or enforce a block until UD is completed?

Any clarification or best practice guidance would be appreciated.

entry.title: Connect LLM to SAP with MCP - Part 1
entry.content:
In this blog I go over the scenario of creating an agent that based on user queries interprets them, translates mails and content to business partner and material, and then creates a sales order in the system. This is part 1, in parts 2 and 3 I will extend this to a Cowork scenario where the human player is removed and I add OAuth2 authentication for full audit capability of the process.

I'm not using fancy integrations with SAP BTP in this scenario because I have several smaller customers that simply don't have that, they still use user and password authentication, no IaS, no integration suite. They just want to use AI with what they have.  I'll upgrade this MCP in follow ups, with the most important thing, user based authentication at scale, which secures audit logs, so stay tuned. 

Not only does this blog show you how AI can managed the process it shows how AI can build the entire thing, being the most important skill today: 

  • Knowing what can be done with AI
  • Knowing what business process works, where the information is, and how to use it. 

I have a video tutorial which makes this easier to watch but in essence the MCP is doing 3 things: 

  • Translating an email to business partner ID using a OData call; 
  • Translating descriptions of material to material ID using local vector database. Here I show you how you can do similarity searches so that the LLM rejects low value results. 
  • Creates the sales order with the transactional data created.

Then I show this MCP integrated with Claude, but it could be any agent, even SAP Joule as you can connect MCP to Joule Skills in the studio. By the way question to the audience, does Joule have an out of the box skill to create sales orders? Comment below to let me know as I get conflicting info. 

Additional important remarks:

  • Claude Code does make some errors still especially with fields in the OData service so I did have to make some fixes, but I never touched the code, I just fed the information it lacked back to it, and it fixed everything.
  • Why didn't I use something like the community OData-Explorer MCP that makes OData services available to LLMs? Honestly, lack of control, LLM still make mistakes when calling APIs and I want to make sure the translation to the API call is safer and more reliable. I also wanted to implement local vector search for efficiency sake and similarity scores so It would be a no go. 

Finally, regarding the infamous API policy I won't go there in this blog. If you believe something like this is against the new policy I think every customer should a talk with their account managers to discuss the situation. I believe some AI use cases do present a risk to ERP stability but things like this are just replicating what a normal external application could also do. 

Comment below if there is a feature you would like me to add to the MCP. 

Thanks for reading

entry.title: ABAP Cleaner
entry.content:
Overview

ABAP Cleaner is an open-source code formatting and cleanup tool for SAP's ABAP programming language.

What it does: It automatically applies  cleanup rules to ABAP code, improving readability, consistency to modern ABAP standards without changing the code's logic.

Challenges

The main challenge with ABAP Cleaner is getting everyone on the team to use it consistently. Different developers have their own coding habits, and agreeing on a single set of rules isn't always easy. There's also a risk when applying it to old legacy code, as unexpected changes can create problems. Additionally, since it only works with ADT (Eclipse), developers who still use SAP GUI cannot benefit from it at all.

Key Features:

  • Over 100+ configurable cleanup rules.
  • Works as a plugin for ABAP Development Tools (ADT) in Eclipse, or as a standalone app.
  • Rules can be individually enabled/disabled and configured to match team standards.
  • Supports interactive preview you see what changes will be made before applying them.
  • Can clean a single file or entire packages/projects at once.

Common Cleanups it performs:

  • Fixes indentation and alignment.
  • Replaces obsolete ABAP syntax with modern equivalents .
  • Standardises spacing around operators and keywords.
  • Removes unnecessary code.
  • Enforces consistent use of DATA, FINAL, FIELD-SYMBOLS, etc.

Requirement

Before installing ABAP Cleaner, make sure you have:

  • Eclipse IDE (latest version recommended)
  • ADT (ABAP Development Tools) already installed in Eclipse

 

Step-by-Step Installation

Step 1:  Open Eclipse with ADT

Launch your Eclipse IDE where ADT is already installed and set up.

 

Step 2: Open Install New Software

Go to the menu bar and click: Help → Install New Software.

 

Step 3: Enter the ABAP Cleaner Update Site

In the "Work with" field at the top, paste the following URL and press Enter:

https://sap.github.io/abap-cleaner/updatesite

Step 4: Select ABAP Cleaner

Wait for the list to load. You will see ABAP Cleaner appear in the list below. Check the checkbox next to it to select it.

Step 5: Click Next

Click Next > to proceed to the installation details screen.

 Step 6:  Review and Click Next Again

Review the items to be installed and click Next > again.

Step 7:  Accept the License Agreement

Read and accept the license agreement by selecting "I accept the terms of the license agreement", then click Finish.

 Step 8: Trust the Content

A security warning may appear saying the content is unsigned. This is expected. Click Install Anyway or Trust Selected to continue.

Step 9:  Restart Eclipse

Once installation completes, Eclipse will prompt you to restart. Click Restart Now to apply the changes.

 

How to Use After Installation.

Once Eclipse restarts, open any ABAP code file and access ABAP Cleaner from:

Right click > source code > You can find installed ABAP cleaner options.

1.Automated Cleaning

Instantly applies all enabled cleanup rules to your code without requiring a preview or confirmation. It simply cleans and is finished.

  • Quick and quiet
  • No opportunity to review modifications prior to application
  •  It works best when you have complete faith in your configured rules.

2. Interactive Cleaning

Opens a window that compares your original code with the cleaned version side by side. Before applying, you have the option to accept or reject specific changes.

  • Complete control over the modifications.

  • Certain rules can be instantly enabled or disabled.
  • A little slower but extremely secure.

 

3. Read-Only Sample

Demonstrates how the cleaned code would appear but makes no use of it. Your code remains unaltered in pure preview mode.

  • No danger, no alteration.
  • Excellent for piqued interest or discovering the functionality of the tool.
  • Beneficial prior to code sharing in reviews.

 

Most ABAPers should default to Interactive Cleanup . It gives you the safety of reviewing changes while still automating the heavy lifting. Only you can switch to Automated once you have configured and trust your cleanup profile fully.

 

ABAP Cleaner read your old-style code on the left, applied modern ABAP rules, and showed the cleaned version on the right.

 

Cleanup Settings Panel

 Two profiles are available:

 

Profiles

What it does

Default

Applies a broad set of rules formatting, case, spacing, Best for full code cleanup

Essential

Applies only the most critical rules minimal, safe changes only. Best for production code.

 

 

 

Profiles & Rules Configuration

The Profiles & Rules page is the most powerful part of ABAP Cleaner it gives full control over which rules apply, How they transform code, and lets teams share consistent cleanup profiles across projects. This is the final step before running the cleaner on your real ABAP codebase.

 

ABAP Cleaner's 100 Rules

ABAP Cleaner's 100 rules are essentially an automated code reviewer that converts years of legacy old-syntax ABAP into clean, readable, modern code in just a few clicks. For any ABAP developer wanting to implement Clean ABAP standards, this is a must have tool.

 

Conclusion

Writing clear and readable code is essential in modern ABAP development. By automatically enhancing outdated code, following to Clean ABAP guidelines, and maintaining consistency, ABAP Cleaner supports developers. Developers can concentrate on creating better solutions instead of spending time manually fixing code. Using ABAP Cleaner supports high code quality over time in along with saving time.

entry.title: Understanding Read Lock Strategy in SMB Sender and Receiver Adapters
entry.content:
In file-based integrations, one of the most common challenges is ensuring that files are processed only after the write operation is fully complete. If a file is picked up while the source system is still writing to it, or if a target system accesses a file while it is still being written by SAP Integration Suite, it can lead to partial payload consumption, file corruption, and downstream processing failures. 

This challenge becomes even more critical when we do not have direct access to the source or target directory to validate how file creation is being handled externally. In such scenarios, the integration flow must rely on adapter-level mechanisms to ensure file stability. 

Consider an invoice-processing integration. 

An external source finance application writes large invoice payloads to a shared SMB directory. SAP Integration Suite polls this directory, processes the payload, applies transformations, and writes the output to another SMB location where the target application consumes it. 

Now imagine two possible situations: 

If SAP picks up a file while the source system is still writing data into it, the integration flow processes an incomplete payload. 

Similarly, if the target application reads the output file while SAP is still writing it, the file may appear malformed or incomplete, resulting in processing failures and incorrect or rejected data at the target system. 

To address this, the SMB Adapter in SAP Integration Suite provides a Read Lock Strategy for both Sender and Receiver adapters. 

Although the end result is the same, ensuring only stable files are processed, the implementation logic differs significantly between sender-side file consumption and receiver-side file writing. 

Read Lock Strategy in the SMB Sender Adapter 

On the sender side, the objective is to ensure that the adapter polls and consumes only fully committed files from the source directory. 

The default read lock option is None. 

In this mode, the adapter consumes the file immediately when detected during polling. This works well only if the external source system fully manages the integrity of the file during creation. 

However, many source systems write directly into the monitored directory. In such cases, the file may become visible to the adapter before the writing is complete. 

To handle this, the Content Change strategy can be used. 

Here, the adapter continuously monitors the file’s last modified timestamp and waits until the file remains unchanged for the configured Content Change Duration (in seconds). 

The validation logic is:  
Current Time - Last Modified Time > Configured Wait Time specified by Content Change Duration field.

In simple terms, the adapter checks whether sufficient time has passed since the last modification. 

If the elapsed time is greater than the configured Content Change Duration, the file is considered stable and ready for processing. 

Configuring this correctly ensures that the source system has completed its write operation before the file is picked up for further processing. 

Read Lock Strategy in the SMB Receiver Adapter 

On the receiver side, the focus is on ensuring that the target system can access the file only after the write operation is complete. The objective here is to ensure that the target system sees the file only after SAP Integration Suite has completed writing to it. 

This read lock strategy is available when Existing File Handling is configured as Override, Fail, or Ignore.

For the read lock strategy, you can choose None, which does not apply any file locking mechanism during file creation. 

Alternatively, the Rename option uses a write-to-temp-first approach. 

Instead of writing directly to the target file, the adapter first writes to a uniquely generated temporary file based on the configured pattern in the Temporary File Name field. 

For example, if the final file name is: Demo.txt and the Temporary File Name is configured as target_${exchangeId}.temp, the adapter creates a file as Demo.txt_AGoK7j2uHH0IrL7dUmZ6wGIR0Omr.temp

 The exchangeId-based suffix ensures uniqueness and avoids conflicts during concurrent processing. Alternatively, you can achieve the same result by using custom formats.

Once the write operation to the temporary file is complete, the adapter checks whether the target file already exists and evaluates the configured Existing File Handling behavior. 

Based on this evaluation, the adapter either renames the temporary file to the final file name, ignores the operation, or fails the processing step, depending on the configured Existing File Handling option. 

This write-to-temp-first approach ensures that the target system never encounters a partially written file. The file becomes available to the target application only after the write operation is fully complete. 

Business Impact 

In file exchange scenarios where file integrity is critical, such as invoice processing, payroll transfers, financial settlements, or master-data synchronization, incomplete files can lead to processing failures, data mismatches, and additional manual effort to correct them.

By enabling sender-side and receiver-side read lock strategies appropriately, file-based integrations become more reliable, reducing operational failures and supporting stable processing for enterprise-scale workloads.

entry.title: Como integrar um serviço BTP Cloud Foundry a um ambiente S4HANA On-premise
entry.content:
O cenário é o seguinte: você quer hospedar um app Fiori Freestyle no BTP Cloud Foundry, que vai se conectar a um sistema S4HANA on-premise e será utilizado pelos usuários finais.

Pode parecer bem específico, mas isso envolve um entendimento de várias camadas de arquitetura, então vale a pena tomar um tempo para entender isso.

Esse objetivo envolve criar serviços auxiliares, que vão se comunicar com o S4HANA por intermédio do Cloud Connector.

O primeiro passo é instalar o SAP Cloud Connector, pode ser no mesmo servidor que hospeda o S4HANA ou em outro servidor na mesma rede.

Ele é um programa Java, então é necessário instalar a JVM. Tecnicamente pode ser usada qualquer JVM, porém a SAP também possui suas próprias versões da JVM, então é recomendável usar elas. Os detalhes estão nesse link: https://help.sap.com/docs/connectivity/sap-btp-connectivity-cf/installation.

Com ele rodando no ambiente, você vai configurar os dados da sua BTP subaccount (ele pode se conectar em mais de uma subaccount).

A conexão entre os serviços da subaccount e o S4HANA vão ser intermediadas por essa conexão com o Cloud Connector.

Quando falamos em integração de serviços externos com um ambiente SAP, não podemos ignorar a parte de autenticação e segurança. Existem várias formas de configuração, mas a mais “nativa” para esse cenário seria a Principal Propagation.

Para entender a arquitetura, mentalize o seguinte fluxo, supondo que no Cloud Foundry você escolha o Node como backend:

  • O usuário abre a aplicação Fiori, que tenta puxar uma informação de uma API do S4HANA através do path relativo (Ex: /sap/opu/odata4/sap/z*);

  • Essa requisição cai no container Node, e se o usuário não estiver logado, ele é redirecionado para uma url de autenticação, onde é informado seu email e senha do Identity Provider (que pode ser da SAP, Azure, AWS, etc.);

  • Essa autenticação gera um token com informações do usuário, que é enviado ao Node;

  • O Node, tendo o token e o caminho da API, direciona ambos para um destination, que aponta para a conexão criada no Cloud Connector;

  • O Cloud Connector pega uma informação desse token, por exemplo o e-mail do usuário, e gera um certificado digital temporário;

  • Esse certificado é enviado em uma conexão https para o endpoint real do S4HANA

  • O S4HANA valida o certificado e mapeia ele para um usuário interno

  • A partir daí a lógica da API odata segue normal, baseada no que o usuário pode fazer, e a resposta é devolvida;

Existe um outro blog post que também explica sobre o Principal Propagation. De qualquer forma vou explicar aqui, mas se quiser conferir, o link é: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/principal-propagation-setup-between-s4hana-and-btp-portal/ba-p/13522753.

Para tornar isso possível, após conectar o Cloud Connector na subaccount, o próximo passo vai ser configurar os certificados digitais dele, na área “Connector > Configuration > On-premises”.

Nesse painel existem 2 tipos de certificado digital: um é para o proxy do Cloud Connector, esse é denominado “System certificate”, e o outro é o que o Cloud Connector utiliza para gerar os certificados temporários, denominado “CA Certificate”.

Nessa mesma página é possível configurar quais informações extrair do token para gerar o certificado, na área do Principal Propagation.

Após isso, é hora de configurar a conexão com o S4HANA on-premise.

Primeiramente, o Cloud Connector precisa confiar no certificado https do servidor S4HANA. Por padrão ele só confia em uma lista definida de certificados, denominada “Back-end Trust Store”. Você pode fazer lá o upload manualmente, ou pode também desativar essa opção, assim ele vai confiar em qualquer certificado do back-end.

Feito isso, na área da sua subaccount, página “Cloud to On-Premises”, vai ser configurado os detalhes específicos do proxy.

A conexão deve ser necessariamente via HTTPS, pois é assim que o Cloud Connector consegue trocar o certificado gerado com o S4HANA de forma nativa através do próprio protocolo.

Exemplo (na edição ele exibe como HTTP, mas foi criada como HTTPS):

Feito isso, a próxima etapa é fazer o Cloud Connector confiar no token gerado pelo serviço de autenticação da sua subaccount.

Para isso, nessa mesma página do “Cloud to On-Premises” existe também a aba do “Principal propagation”, onde você deve sincronizar. A primeira execução é manual, depois dela você pode marcar a opção para sincronizar automaticamente.

 

Em resumo, até aqui já temos uma instância do Cloud Connector rodando na rede, vinculada a uma subaccount BTP, com os certificados system e CA criados, e configurada para se conectar ao S4HANA on-premise via https.

Agora faltam 2 coisas: configurar o S4HANA para confiar no Cloud Connector, e configurar os serviços no BTP Cloud Foundry para que seja possível essa integração.

Para que o S4HANA confie no Cloud Connector, é necessário ajustar alguns parâmetros de perfil do sistema (isso envolve configurações BASIS).

O primeiro ponto é fazer ele confiar no certificado digital de sistema do Cloud Connector, existem 2 formas de fazer isso:

  • Com os antigos parâmetros “icm/HTTPS/trust_client_with_subject” e “icm/HTTPS/trust_client_with_issuer”, que são configurados via transação RZ10. Esses parâmetros definem o padrão do emissor e assunto do certificado digital que o sistema pode confiar, e é um valor único. Eles são do tipo estático, então é necessário reiniciar a instância do S4 para aplicar as edições.

Exemplo:

  • Com o novo parâmetro “icm/trusted_reverse_proxy_<x>”, onde o “<x>” é um número sequencial começando do 0. Ele tem esse padrão porque dessa forma é possível configurar mais de um certificado confiado pelo servidor, diferente dos anteriores que só permitiam um valor. Ele é do tipo dinâmico, então não é sempre necessário reiniciar a instância para aplicar, e pode ser alterado a partir da transação RZ11 (de forma indireta). Mais informações sobre ele no link: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_NETWEAVER_AS_ABAP_752/077f95b579a0474fbef5d520be10b680/3bea0adde9a54db5843be8f7bf01fefa.html.

Exemplo:

Também é necessário importar os dois certificados do Cloud Connector na transação STRUST, no servidor SSL Padrão.

Feito isso, o sistema S4HANA confia no certificado do Cloud Connector, mas ainda não consegue mapear os certificados temporários para usuários dentro do SAP. Aí entra a configuração da transação CERTRULE, onde você faz o upload de um certificado temporário gerado pelo Cloud Connector e pode mapear ele para usuários através de regras, ou diretamente.

Exemplo:

Para habilitar isso também é necessário configurar um parâmetro de perfil, o “login/certificate_mapping_rulebased”:

No lado do S4HANA é basicamente isso, agora só falta configurar os serviços dentro do BTP.

O cenário pensado nesse tutorial é de uma aplicação CAP Fiori rodando no Cloud Foudry, então vamos criar os serviços no mesmo space da aplicação.

São basicamente 3 serviços:

  • destination: Conceito similar ao da transação SM59, guarda dentro dele o destino para a conexão do Cloud Connector. Ele possui painel próprio para configurações.

  • connectivity: É um serviço sem configuração nenhuma, que apenas serve como ponte entre a aplicação e o serviço de destination.

  • xsuaa: É o que faz a ponte da aplicação com o provedor de identidade. Ele é configurado apenas via json, onde é possível definir o app que vai consumi-lo, as urls que pode redirecionar após autenticar o usuário, e até mesmo criar novas roles dentro do BTP, que podem posteriormente ser incluídas em Role collections e atribuídas a usuários.

Feito isso, só falta configurar a conexão dentro do painel do “Destination Service”. Lá você vai criar um destination com o Proxy type “OnPremise” e mapear para a url virtual gerada no painel do Cloud Connector, com autenticação via “PrincipalPropagation”.

Exemplo:

O app node deve estar configurado para que o destino do S4HANA seja esse Name que você configurou acima (não confunda isso com a url virtual ou com o serviço destination).

Agora para integrar tudo isso, basta a aplicação estar configurada para fazer o binding nesses 3 serviços. Isso é feito através do arquivo “manifest.yml”, usado na hora de subir o app no Cloud Foundry.

Exemplo:

Finalmente, seu app Node estará pronto para se autenticar e consumir serviços odata do seu S4HANA On-Premise.

 

Em resumo, ao seguir esse tutorial você foi capaz de entender e configurar as camadas de integração entre um app no BTP Cloud Foundry e um sistema S4Hana On-Premise (via api OData).

Talvez essas ferramentas, configurações e caminhos específicos mudem com o tempo, o mais importante é entender a arquitetura e os conceitos envolvidos nessa integração.

Obrigado pela leitura, espero ter ajudado 😄

entry.title: SAP Joule Document Grounding with Google Drive - Connecting AI to Enterprise Knowledge (Video Demo)
entry.content:
Enterprise AI becomes significantly more powerful when it can access organization-specific knowledge instead of relying only on generic model responses.

With document grounding in SAP Joule, Joule can retrieve information from enterprise repositories such as Google Drive or SharePoint and generate contextual, reliable responses using Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG).

In this blog, I’ll demonstrate how to connect SAP Joule with Google Drive and enable document grounding using SAP BTP.


What is Document Grounding?

Document grounding allows Joule to retrieve enterprise documents before generating responses.

This helps:

  • Improve response accuracy
  • Reduce hallucinations
  • Provide organization-specific answers
  • Show source references for transparency

Typical grounded content includes:

  • SOPs
  • Functional specifications
  • HR policies
  • IT procedures
  • Internal process documentation

Demo Scenario

For this demo:

  • Google Drive is used as the document repository
  • SAP Joule is connected through the Document Grounding service
  • A RAG pipeline indexes enterprise documents
  • Joule retrieves information directly from grounded documents

I tested two Joule instances:

  • One without grounding
  • One with document grounding enabled

Steps to Enable Document Grounding in SAP Joule

Prerequisites

Ensure you have:

  • Access to SAP Joule
  • Document Grounding service entitlement
  • Google Cloud access
  • Google Drive repository containing documents

Step 1 — Subscribe to SAP Joule and Grounding Service

In your SAP BTP subaccount:

  1. Subscribe to:
    • SAP Joule
    • Document Grounding service

  2. Once the grounding service is available:
    • Create a service instance
    • Generate a service key

The service key will be used for authentication while creating and managing the RAG pipeline.

Step 2 — Create a Google Cloud Service User

In Google Cloud Platform:

  1. Create a Service Account
  2. Generate credentials for the service account
  3. Download the JSON credentials file

This service user will allow the grounding service to access Google Drive content securely.

Step 3 — Create a Google Drive Folder and Grant Access

  1. Create a dedicated folder in Google Drive

Example:
SAPJouleDataGrounding

  1. Upload the documents you want Joule to access:
    • PDFs
    • PPTs
    • Text files
    • Images

  2. Grant View access on the folder to the Google Cloud service user created earlier.

This allows the grounding pipeline to index and retrieve content from the repository.

Step 4 — Create and Verify the Grounding Pipeline

Using Postman or any API testing tool:

  1. Authenticate using the grounding service key
  2. Create a new grounding/RAG pipeline
  3. Configure:
    • Google Drive as the repository
    • Folder details
    • Service account credentials
  4. Activate the pipeline

Once created, verify the pipeline status.

In my demo, the pipeline successfully served content from the Google Drive folder, confirming:

  • Repository connectivity
  • Successful indexing

Step 5 — Test in SAP Joule

Now launch SAP Joule and ask organization-specific questions.

Example:

“What’s the process to raise SE38 FFID?”

or

“Tell me about Auralis custom process for vendor onboarding.”

With document grounding enabled, Joule retrieves the relevant enterprise documents and generates contextual responses along with source references

Demo Video

I have also included a dedicated demo video showcasing:

  • End-to-end setup of document grounding
  • Google Drive integration
  • RAG pipeline creation and verification
  • Grounded vs ungrounded Joule responses
  • Enterprise knowledge retrieval in action using SAP Joule

The video provides a complete walkthrough of the configuration process along with a live demonstration of grounded AI responses.

Thank you for reading this blog.

I will also be creating more content around custom SAP Joule Agents using Joule Studio and sharing additional demos, integrations, and enterprise AI use cases here.

Stay tuned for more SAP Joule and AI-related content.

entry.title: Re: 3747694 - ABAP SAPUI5 1.120.45 : /UI5/MIME_CLEANUP fa...
entry.content:
Hello 

Check SM12 → delete locks for /UI5/* or MIME.
Check SE03 → unlock objects (LIMU MIME) or release related transports.
Ensure no active UI5 usage / jobs.
Run /UI5/MIME_CLEANUP again in background.
If still failing → use SE03 → Unlock Objects (Expert tool) to force unlock.

In most cases, clearing SM12 + SE03 locks fixes it.

Thanks,
Sravan

entry.title: Re: New asset created but depreciation is calculated for...
entry.content:
Depreciation calculation formula:

yearly depreciation=base value*period*percentage

If asset useful life is less than 1 year, the percentage in the first year will be calculated as useful life/useful life=1.

For deeper reference, the following KBAs outline the logic and examples:

- 2579444 Depreciation Calculation when the Useful Life is less than 1 year

- 3349933 How the depreciation rate is calculated when the remaining useful life indicator is set and useful life is less than 1 year

Base value is calculated base on your depreciation key, multilevel method customizing.

"Display Depreciation Calculation" icon can be found in Asset Explorer (Tx: AW01N) under Planned Values tab. Please use this functionality to analyze the Depreciation calculation.

Additionally please check Guided Help for Depreciation Calculation that you can find in the following to support your analysis:

2614154- Guide for understanding the ordinary depreciation calculation amounts - Guided Answers

entry.title: Text Processing in NLP
entry.content:
Introduction:

NLP is a field of Artificial Intelligence (AI) that allows computers to understand, interpret, and utilize human language in context. The reason human language is so complicated is primarily due to grammar, emotions, context words and terminologies of a particular field as well as different writing styles. The rise of Natural language processing:NLP enables machines to dissect this language and transmute unstructured text into structured data, which is easily consumable by computers.

Body:

Natural Language Processing employs a number of textual preprocessing methodologies including tokenization, lemmatization, vectorization and similarity modeling. These techniques are used to split the text into smaller pieces (such as words or sentences), extract root words, convert that text into a numerical vector and compare meaning between two sentences/sentences/documents. Further, after the data is processed it can feed machine learning and deep learning models for intelligent decision making and predictions.

Tokenization :

Tokenization breaks down a given sentence or text into smaller parts called tokens. Computers cannot read human sentence as a whole. Hence, first NLP divides the text into smaller chunks for processing.

Example:

Input: “NLP is very interesting”

Output : ["NLP", "is", "very", "interesting"]

Lemmatization

Lemmatization is the process of converting a word to its base or root form known as a lemma. This allows NLP systems to comprehend that different variants of a term signify the same thing.

Input :["running", "studies", "better"]

Output : ["run", "study", "good"]

Vectorization

Vectorization is the process of converting text or words into numerical form (vectors) so that machine learning models can understand and process them.TF-IDF Vectorization is an NLP technique used to convert text into numerical vectors based on the importance of words in a document. TF-IDF helps identify which words are important and meaningful in a sentence or document

Input

["I love NLP", "NLP is interesting"]

Output : 

[

 [1, 1, 1, 0],

 [0, 1, 0, 1]

]

Find similarity:

In NLP, finding similarity is the task of two texts to see how similar they are.It teaches computers to understand when two sentences are saying the same thing.Word Embeddings, Cosine Similarity and TF-IDF are commonly used techniques. Typically, the similarity score is between 0 and 1.

Input

Sentence 1: "I love NLP"

Sentence 2: "I like NLP"

 

Output

0.82

 

Summary: 

NLP is widely used these days in chatbots, virtual assistants, search engines, translation systems, sentiment analysis, recommendation systems, spam filtering, and AI-powered enterprise applications. Popular applications include Siri, Alexa, Google Translate, ChatGPT and customer service bots. NLP is a key factor in enhancing human-computer interaction and automating language-based tasks in different sectors.

entry.title: Re: How to disable Manager and Self Assignment of items w...
entry.content:
Hi @c_montag-26 

Removing the Manage Assignments' permisison from the manager's role will prevent managers from seeing or accessing Manage Assignments in Team View -> more manager action menu. This option is what managers use to assign Learning and to Remove Assigned Learning for their subordinates.

Team View
- Manage Assignments

Removing the 'Add to Learning Assignments' permission from a user's role will prevent that user from assigning any item to self. The Assigned to me option may still appear on the item, but when the user tries to assign the item to self, it will display the message: You do not have permission to perform this action'.

Learning
- Add to Learning Assignments

For additional details, you may review the attached KBA 2461827  and see if it meets your requirement.

entry.title: Re: Why does SAP allow MIRO posting for stock in Quality...
entry.content:
Why SAP allows MIRO posting during Quality Inspection is because of the following:

1) Invoice Verification Independence
MIRO is part of Materials Management (MM) and focuses on matching PO, GR, and invoice values. It does not depend on Quality Management (QM) inspection status.

2) Design Reasoning
SAP assumes businesses may need to record vendor invoices promptly for financial accuracy, even if goods are still under inspection. Blocking invoice posting could delay accounting and distort liabilities.

3) QM Procurement Controls
QM Procurement Key and Active QM in Procurement can trigger a payment block, not a posting block. This ensures invoices are recorded but payments are withheld until inspection is complete.

Having said that if compliance requires strict blocking, implement custom validation in MIRO to prevent posting when stock is in Quality Inspection. If financial accuracy is priority, SAP standard — post invoice, block payment until UD.

entry.title: Re: ME59N_request input&advice if enhance ME59N for n...
entry.content:
Technical Concerns

  1. Standard Grouping Logic
    • ME59N groups requisitions primarily by vendor, purchasing organization, purchasing group, plant, and account assignment.
    • Introducing “Material No.” as a grouping criterion changes the fundamental logic — you’d be overriding SAP’s standard grouping rules.
  2. Multiple Vendors / Sources of Supply
    • A single material can have multiple info records or sources of supply.
    • Grouping “per material” risks combining requisitions that belong to different vendors, which is not valid in SAP PO creation.
  3. Different Purchasing Data
    • Even for the same material, requisitions may differ in:
      • Delivery dates
      • Plants/storage locations
      • Account assignments (cost center, WBS, etc.)
    • Grouping them into one PO line could break downstream processes (MRP, GR, invoice matching).
  4. Pricing & Conditions
    • SAP pricing is vendor‑specific. Grouping by material alone ignores vendor conditions, scales, and discounts.
    • You’d need custom logic to ensure the correct vendor and condition record is applied.
  5. Performance Impact
    • ME59N is already performance‑sensitive. Adding custom grouping logic (especially material‑based aggregation) could slow down batch runs significantly.
  6. Maintenance & Upgrade Risk
    • ME59N is a standard transaction. Enhancing it directly (via modification) is risky:
      • Future upgrades or support packs may overwrite your changes.
      • SAP generally discourages modifying core transactions; BAdIs or enhancements are safer.

Best Practice Alternatives

  • Use Source List / Info Records: Ensure requisitions for the same material point to the same vendor, then ME59N will naturally group them.
  • Use Scheduling Agreements: For high‑volume materials, scheduling agreements capture aggregated demand and volume discounts better than grouping POs.
  • Custom Report / Program: Instead of enhancing ME59N, build a custom program that aggregates requisitions per material/vendor, then creates POs via BAPI. This avoids modifying standard logic.
